physicalExam.reasoning: Hemodynamic instability

physicalExam.reasoning: Airway compromise

labs.name: Serum tryptase (draw within 3 hours of onset)
labs.result: Elevated at 45 ng/mL (normal <11.4 ng/mL). CONFIRMS ANAPHYLAXIS. Draw at time of reaction, 1-2 hours later, and baseline at 24 hours.
labs.reasoning: Tryptase confirms mast cell degranulation. Draw DURING resuscitation, don't delay treatment. Peak at 1-2 hours, returns to baseline by 24 hours. Helps distinguish anaphylaxis from other causes of shock.

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.32, pCO2 28, pO2 68, HCO3 18, lactate 3.2 - metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation, elevated lactate indicating tissue hypoperfusion
labs.reasoning: Assesses oxygenation and acid-base status during anaphylactic shock. Elevated lactate indicates inadequate tissue perfusion.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 14.2, Hgb 12.8, Plt 198. Eosinophils 8% (elevated).
labs.reasoning: Eosinophilia may be present in allergic conditions. Also baseline for monitoring.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.8, Cr 1.2 (baseline 0.9). Glucose 186 (stress response).
labs.reasoning: Renal function for medication dosing. Hyperkalemia can occur with tissue hypoperfusion. Stress hyperglycemia common.

labs.name: Latex-specific IgE (post-recovery, not during acute event)
labs.result: Positive - high level (>100 kU/L). Confirms latex sensitization.
labs.reasoning: Send AFTER patient stable. Confirms latex as causative allergen for future avoidance.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.result: Troponin I 0.08 ng/mL (mildly elevated).
labs.reasoning: Anaphylaxis can cause Kounis syndrome (allergic MI) from coronary vasospasm or demand ischemia. Check if chest pain or ECG changes.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (portable)
imaging.result: Bilateral diffuse interstitial edema consistent with non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (capillary leak). No pneumothorax.
imaging.reasoning: Assess for pulmonary edema from capillary leak, aspiration if vomiting occurred, or complications from airway management.

imaging.name: 12-lead ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 128 bpm. Nonspecific ST-T changes. No acute STEMI pattern.
imaging.reasoning: Rule out Kounis syndrome (allergic MI). Tachycardia expected. Watch for arrhythmias especially with epinephrine administration.

procedures.name: Large-bore IV access x 2
procedures.result: Two 18-gauge IVs placed in bilateral antecubital fossae.
procedures.reasoning: CRITICAL for fluid resuscitation and medication administration. Large bore essential for rapid volume infusion in anaphylactic shock.

procedures.name: Endotracheal intubation
procedures.result: Patient intubated for airway protection due to angioedema and declining mental status.
procedures.reasoning: EARLY INTUBATION for progressive angioedema - airway can become impossible to manage if delayed. Video laryngoscope and surgical airway backup must be ready.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Placed. UOP 15 mL in first hour, improved to 45 mL/hr after resuscitation.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor urine output as marker of tissue perfusion during resuscitation.

treatments.name: Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (anterolateral thigh)
treatments.result: Epinephrine 0.5 mg IM given immediately upon recognition. May repeat every 5-15 minutes.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis. IM to anterolateral thigh (NOT deltoid, NOT subcutaneous). Delays in epinephrine are associated with fatality. Repeat every 5-15 minutes if no response.

treatments.name: Epinephrine IV infusion (for refractory shock)
treatments.result: Epinephrine infusion started at 0.1 mcg/kg/min, titrated to MAP >65.
treatments.reasoning: For anaphylaxis refractory to IM epinephrine or with cardiovascular collapse. Continuous infusion allows titration. Monitor for arrhythmias.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L NS bolus, then continued at 500 mL/hr. Third-spacing and vasodilation require aggressive volume.
treatments.reasoning: Massive fluid shifts occur in anaphylaxis (up to 35% of intravascular volume in 10 minutes). Aggressive crystalloid resuscitation essential.

treatments.name: REMOVE ALL LATEX from environment
treatments.result: ALL latex products removed from OR including gloves, tourniquets, IV tubing. Latex-free products obtained.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL - continued antigen exposure worsens reaction. Remove all latex from room, switch to latex-free gloves and equipment.

treatments.name: Albuterol nebulizer for bronchospasm
treatments.result: Albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized continuously for bronchospasm.
treatments.reasoning: For bronchospasm not responding to epinephrine. Continuous nebulization may be needed.

treatments.name: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV
treatments.result: Given for urticaria and as H1 blocker.
treatments.reasoning: H1 blocker for urticaria/itching. Does NOT treat bronchospasm or hypotension - epinephrine is the primary treatment.

treatments.name: Famotidine 20 mg IV (H2 blocker)
treatments.result: Given along with H1 blocker.
treatments.reasoning: H2 blocker may provide additional histamine blockade. Evidence limited but commonly used as adjunct.

treatments.name: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
treatments.result: Given to prevent biphasic reaction.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids do NOT treat acute anaphylaxis but may prevent biphasic reactions (occur in 5-20% at 1-72 hours). Give after epinephrine.

treatments.name: Glucagon 1-5 mg IV (for patients on beta-blockers)
treatments.result: Not needed - patient not on beta-blockers.
treatments.reasoning: For patients on beta-blockers who may not respond to epinephrine. Glucagon has positive inotropic and chronotropic effects independent of beta-receptors.

treatments.name: Vasopressin (for refractory shock)
treatments.result: Added vasopressin 0.04 units/min for refractory hypotension.
treatments.reasoning: Second-line vasopressor for anaphylactic shock refractory to epinephrine and fluids.

consults.name: Anesthesiology (emergent airway management)
consults.result: Anesthesia at bedside for airway management. Video laryngoscope and surgical airway equipment ready.
consults.reasoning: Expert airway management essential - angioedema can progress rapidly making intubation impossible.

consults.name: Allergy/Immunology (post-recovery)
consults.result: Referral placed. Will confirm latex allergy with skin testing and specific IgE. Medical alert documentation and bracelet ordered.
consults.reasoning: Confirm culprit allergen, provide counseling on avoidance, prescribe epinephrine auto-injector, arrange for medical alert identification.

consults.name: Occupational Health
consults.result: Referral for workplace accommodation - patient cannot work with latex products.
consults.reasoning: Healthcare worker with latex allergy needs workplace accommodation and possible job modification.

disposition.name: Admit to ICU for observation
disposition.result: Admitted to ICU for 24-48 hour observation for biphasic reaction.
disposition.reasoning: All patients with anaphylaxis requiring epinephrine should be observed 4-24 hours for biphasic reaction. ICU for patients with severe reaction, intubation, or ongoing vasopressor requirement.

disposition.name: Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector at discharge
disposition.result: EpiPen 0.3 mg x 2 prescribed. Training provided on use.
disposition.reasoning: ALL patients with anaphylaxis need epinephrine auto-injector prescription and training before discharge.

closureScript.diagnosis: The patient had a severe allergic reaction to latex - the material in surgical gloves and other medical equipment. Her years of working as an OR nurse sensitized her to latex.
closureScript.plan: We've treated the anaphylaxis and removed all latex from the OR. She needs to be in a completely latex-free environment for recovery. She'll need allergy testing to confirm and a medical alert bracelet.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Any future medical procedures must be latex-free. This includes dental work, any surgeries, even simple procedures like Foley catheter placement. The allergy is permanent.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: She'll need to inform her employer - she cannot work with latex products anymore. Most hospitals now have latex-free alternatives available.

male-42-beesting.oldcartsNarrative:
"I was trimming hedges at a client's house 3 weeks ago when I got stung on my hand by a honeybee. I could see the stinger so I scraped it out. Within about 10 minutes, I broke out in hives all over my body - not just near the sting. My lips started tingling and my throat felt tight. I got really dizzy and lightheaded. My coworker said I looked pale and sweaty. He called 911 and the paramedics gave me a shot - I think epinephrine - and Benadryl. They took me to the ER where they watched me for a few hours and sent me home with an EpiPen.

I've been stung before - maybe 5-6 times over the years working outside. Usually I just get a big red swelling at the sting site that lasts a few days. But nothing like this ever happened before. My doctor said this was anaphylaxis and I need to see an allergist about allergy shots.

I'm nervous because I work outside every day. I can't really avoid bees. My wife is scared that the next sting might kill me. I always carry my EpiPen now but I want to know if there's anything else I can do."

questions.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

female-45-penicillin.oldcartsNarrative:
"I've had 'penicillin allergy' listed in my chart since I was a kid - maybe 5 or 6 years old. My mom told me I got a rash when I was on amoxicillin for an ear infection. I don't remember it at all - I just know it's been on my medical records forever.

Every time I need antibiotics, the doctors have to use something different. Last year I had a sinus infection and they gave me azithromycin. The year before, I had a UTI and got ciprofloxacin. My dentist always gives me clindamycin before procedures because I have a heart murmur.

I'm asking about this now because I'm scheduled for a knee replacement in a few months. The orthopedic surgeon said penicillin-type antibiotics are the best for preventing bone infections, and he recommended I see an allergist to find out if I'm really allergic.

I've never had any reaction to anything else - no food allergies, no problems with other medications. I take ibuprofen without any issues. I'm honestly not even sure I was ever really allergic. My mom passed away a few years ago so I can't ask her what exactly happened."

procedures.name: Penicillin Skin Testing (Prick then Intradermal)
procedures.result: NEGATIVE: Penicilloyl-polylysine (Pre-Pen) - negative at prick and intradermal. Minor determinant mixture - negative. Penicillin G - negative. Ampicillin - negative. Histamine positive control: 8mm wheal. Saline negative control: no reaction.
procedures.reasoning: Negative skin testing has >95% negative predictive value. Safe to proceed to oral challenge.

procedures.name: Oral Amoxicillin Challenge
procedures.result: Graded oral challenge performed: 1/10 dose (50mg) → observed 30 min → no reaction. Full dose (500mg) → observed 60 min → no reaction. Patient tolerated amoxicillin without any symptoms.
procedures.reasoning: Oral challenge confirms tolerance after negative skin testing. Provides psychological reassurance and documents tolerance.

actualDiagnosis.name: No True Penicillin Allergy - Successfully Delabeled
teachingPoints: 90% of patients labeled 'penicillin allergic' are NOT truly allergic
IgE-mediated penicillin allergy wanes over time - 50% lose it within 5 years, 80% within 10 years
Penicillin skin testing has >95% negative predictive value
Vague childhood 'rash' without systemic symptoms is very low risk
Many childhood 'reactions' were actually viral exanthems mistaken for drug allergy
Delabeling benefits: optimal antibiotics, fewer C. diff infections, shorter hospital stays, lower costs
Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is low (~1-2%) and based on side chain similarity, not the beta-lactam ring
After negative testing and challenge, patients can safely receive all penicillins and cephalosporins

female-45-penicillin.teachingPoint: Penicillin allergy delabeling is one of the highest-yield interventions in allergy. Steps: (1) Take detailed history - most are low risk, (2) Skin test with major and minor determinants, (3) If negative, perform oral challenge, (4) If tolerant, delabel and document extensively, (5) Communicate to all providers.
female-45-penicillin.clinicalPearl: The childhood 'amoxicillin rash' is often actually a viral exanthem (especially with EBV/mononucleosis) that was coincidentally present during antibiotic treatment. Aminopenicillins cause a characteristic morbilliform rash in patients with active EBV infection - this is NOT a true allergy and does not predict future reactions.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the skin test was positive?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same patient, but skin testing shows positive reaction

chiefComplaint.modified: Same history, but skin testing shows 8mm wheal to penicilloyl-polylysine

keyDiscriminators: Positive skin test confirms IgE-mediated allergy

feedback.correct: Positive skin test = true IgE-mediated allergy. Do NOT challenge. Document confirmed allergy. Discuss alternatives: aztreonam (no cross-reactivity), most cephalosporins safe (side chain dependent), carbapenems usually tolerated.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of positive test significance. What are the antibiotic alternatives?
feedback.incorrect: Positive penicillin skin test confirms IgE-mediated allergy. Do NOT proceed to oral challenge - high risk of reaction. Instead: document confirmed allergy, counsel on avoidance, discuss safe alternatives. Consider desensitization if penicillin absolutely needed.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Negative skin test = proceed to challenge, likely delabel. Positive skin test = confirmed allergy, do NOT challenge, discuss alternatives or desensitization if needed.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if history included anaphylaxis?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient describes throat swelling, epinephrine use after penicillin

chiefComplaint.modified: Patient states: 'My throat swelled shut and I needed an EpiPen shot 15 years ago after taking amoxicillin'

feedback.correct: Severe history (anaphylaxis, epinephrine use) increases pre-test probability but doesn't preclude testing. Skin testing still appropriate and often negative even with anaphylaxis history (sensitivity wanes). If skin test negative, more cautious graded challenge protocol. Many with 'anaphylaxis' history can still be delabeled.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of higher risk history. Can we still test?
feedback.incorrect: Even severe historical reactions can be tested - allergy wanes over time. Skin test first. If negative, use more conservative graded challenge protocol. If positive, confirm allergy and discuss alternatives or desensitization. Don't assume lifelong allergy without testing.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Vague history = very low risk, straightforward testing. Anaphylaxis history = higher pre-test probability, still testable, more cautious challenge protocol if skin test negative.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if patient needed penicillin urgently?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient admitted with endocarditis requiring penicillin/ampicillin

chiefComplaint.modified: Patient admitted with Enterococcal endocarditis. ID says ampicillin is required for optimal treatment. No safe alternative.

feedback.correct: When penicillin is REQUIRED and no alternative exists, use desensitization protocol. Temporary tolerance induced by starting with micro-doses and gradually increasing. ICU setting, continuous infusion after achieving tolerance. Tolerance lost if drug stopped >24-48h.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of desensitization need. What are the logistics?
feedback.incorrect: DESENSITIZATION is the answer when penicillin is medically required and no alternative exists. Protocol involves micro-doses with doubling every 15-30 minutes until therapeutic dose. Done in ICU. Creates TEMPORARY tolerance - must maintain continuous drug exposure. If stopped, need to repeat desensitization.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Routine evaluation = test and potentially delabel. Urgent need with no alternative = desensitization protocol to induce temporary tolerance.

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination

Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

keyPoints: Chronic urticaria defined as daily or near-daily hives for ≥6 weeks

Identify and treat underlying causes if present (autoimmune, infection, etc.)

options.explanation: Correct! Allergy testing in CSU is typically negative or shows sensitizations unrelated to urticaria. Guidelines recommend AGAINST routine allergy testing in chronic urticaria.

allDiagnoses.name: Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Daily hives for >6 weeks with no identifiable trigger. Most common cause of chronic urticaria.

allDiagnoses.name: Chronic Inducible Urticaria
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have consistent physical trigger (cold, pressure, vibration, etc.). No consistent trigger here.

allDiagnoses.name: Autoimmune Thyroid Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: 30% of CSU patients have thyroid autoantibodies. Check TSH and anti-TPO.

allDiagnoses.name: Urticarial Vasculitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Individual lesions lasting >24 hours, painful/burning rather than itchy, leaving bruising. Needs skin biopsy.

allDiagnoses.name: Systemic Mastocytosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: If elevated tryptase or other systemic symptoms (flushing, GI, hypotension).

female-35-hives.oldcartsNarrative:
"I've had hives every single day for about 3 months now. They just appear out of nowhere - on my arms, legs, stomach, back - really anywhere. They're red, raised, and incredibly itchy. Each spot usually lasts a few hours then fades, but new ones keep popping up. I've tried everything to figure out what's causing them.

I've changed my laundry detergent, my soap, my shampoo. I've cut out dairy, gluten, even tried eliminating everything but plain chicken and rice for two weeks - nothing helped. The hives kept coming. I've been taking Benadryl every night just to sleep, but I'm so groggy the next day I can barely function at work.

I went to urgent care twice and they gave me steroid shots which helped for a few days, but then the hives came right back. My primary care doctor did some blood tests but said everything was normal. She referred me to you.

This is ruining my life. I can't concentrate at work. I'm embarrassed to wear short sleeves. I'm exhausted from not sleeping well. I feel like I'm allergic to something but I can't figure out what. I keep thinking there must be something in my house or my food that's doing this."

historyQuestions.text: How long have you been having hives?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 months now - every single day. It started kind of gradually but for the last 2 months it's been daily.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ≥6 weeks duration defines chronic urticaria. Daily occurrence typical of CSU.

historyQuestions.text: How long does each individual hive last?
historyQuestions.answer: Usually a few hours - maybe 2-6 hours. Then it fades completely, no marks left behind. But new ones keep appearing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Individual lesions <24 hours = typical urticaria. >24 hours with bruising = urticarial vasculitis (needs biopsy).

historyQuestions.text: What do the hives look like?
historyQuestions.answer: Red, raised welts. Different sizes - some small like a quarter, some bigger and they merge together. They're really itchy, not painful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic urticaria description - itchy wheals. Painful/burning suggests vasculitis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you identified any triggers?
historyQuestions.answer: That's what's so frustrating - I can't find any pattern! I've kept a diary, changed everything, eliminated foods. They just come randomly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No identifiable trigger is defining feature of CSU. Patient has already done extensive self-investigation.

historyQuestions.text: Do they come on with pressure, cold, heat, or exercise?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really - I've paid attention to that. Sometimes they seem worse when I'm stressed but they happen even when I'm relaxed too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No consistent physical trigger rules out inducible urticarias (cold, pressure, cholinergic, etc.).

historyQuestions.text: If you scratch your skin, does it swell up?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, actually! If I scratch my arm, I get a raised red line that lasts a while.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Symptomatic dermatographism often coexists with CSU - confirms mast cell hyperreactivity.

historyQuestions.text: Any swelling of your lips, eyes, tongue, or throat?
historyQuestions.answer: My lips have puffed up a couple times and my eyes got swollen once. No throat swelling though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Angioedema occurs in 40% of CSU patients - part of the same mast cell process.

historyQuestions.text: Any fever, joint pain, fatigue, or other symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm tired all the time but I think that's from poor sleep because of the itching. No fever or joint pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Systemic symptoms would suggest urticarial vasculitis or other systemic disease.

historyQuestions.text: What are you currently taking for the hives?
historyQuestions.answer: Benadryl every night - it's the only way I can sleep. During the day I've tried Claritin and Zyrtec but they don't seem strong enough.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Already using antihistamines but needs optimization - avoid Benadryl, uptitrate second-gen.

historyQuestions.text: How much Zyrtec or Claritin were you taking?
historyQuestions.answer: One a day, like the box says.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Standard dose - has not tried uptitrated dosing (up to 4x) which is next step.

historyQuestions.text: How did you respond to the steroid shots?
historyQuestions.answer: They worked great - within a day the hives were gone. But they came back about a week after each shot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Steroid-responsive confirms inflammatory/mast cell process but chronic steroids not appropriate.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any thyroid problems?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom has thyroid issues - Hashimoto's I think. I've never been tested.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of autoimmune thyroid + chronic urticaria = should check TSH and anti-TPO.

historyQuestions.text: Any autoimmune conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I know of. My mom has Hashimoto's and my aunt has lupus.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of autoimmune disease increases suspicion for autoimmune etiology of CSU.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: I had seasonal allergies as a kid but grew out of them. Nothing else major.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline health - atopic history common but doesn't cause chronic urticaria.

historyQuestions.text: What other medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. And the Benadryl.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Review for potential drug causes - NSAIDs can worsen urticaria.

historyQuestions.text: Do you take ibuprofen, aspirin, or other pain relievers?
historyQuestions.answer: Occasionally ibuprofen for headaches - maybe once a week.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs can worsen chronic urticaria in some patients - may need to avoid.

historyQuestions.text: Any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: Just my wisdom teeth out in college.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history for completeness.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent infections - sinus, dental, stomach bug?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a stomach bug about 4 months ago, before this all started. Nothing since.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Infections can trigger CSU onset. H. pylori association reported but controversial.

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your sleep?
historyQuestions.answer: Terrible. I wake up scratching. Even with Benadryl I don't sleep well. I'm exhausted.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Quality of life impact drives treatment urgency. CSU significantly affects sleep.

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your work and daily life?
historyQuestions.answer: I can barely concentrate. I'm embarrassed to go to meetings when my arms are covered in hives. It's affecting everything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional impairment - supports need for effective treatment.

physicalExam.reasoning: Arthritis would suggest vasculitis or systemic disease.

labs.name: CBC with Differential
labs.result: WBC 7.8, Hgb 13.5, Plt 265. Normal differential. No eosinophilia.
labs.reasoning: Part of limited recommended workup. Eosinophilia might suggest parasitic or allergic etiology.

labs.name: ESR and CRP
labs.result: ESR 12 mm/hr (normal), CRP 0.3 mg/dL (normal).
labs.reasoning: Normal inflammatory markers - argues against vasculitis or systemic inflammatory disease.

labs.name: TSH
labs.result: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (normal).
labs.reasoning: Normal TSH but should still check thyroid antibodies given CSU association.

labs.name: Anti-TPO Antibodies
labs.result: Elevated at 245 IU/mL (normal <35). Positive for thyroid autoimmunity.
labs.reasoning: Positive anti-TPO confirms autoimmune thyroid disease association. Common in CSU - does not change treatment but explains etiology.

labs.name: Allergy Skin Testing
labs.result: NOT RECOMMENDED for chronic spontaneous urticaria. Guidelines advise against routine allergy testing.
labs.reasoning: Allergy testing is NOT recommended in CSU - rarely identifies cause and leads to unnecessary avoidance.

procedures.name: Skin Biopsy
procedures.result: Not indicated unless lesions last >24 hours or have atypical features suggesting vasculitis.
procedures.reasoning: Biopsy reserved for suspected urticarial vasculitis (lesions >24h, painful, bruising).

treatments.name: Uptitrate Cetirizine to 20mg BID (4x standard dose)
treatments.result: Patient started on cetirizine 20mg twice daily. Counseled that this is safe and evidence-based. Stop Benadryl.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment per guidelines. Uptitration to 4x dose before adding other therapies.

treatments.name: Discontinue First-Generation Antihistamines
treatments.result: Benadryl discontinued. Second-generation antihistamines are preferred - less sedation, no anticholinergic effects.
treatments.reasoning: First-gen antihistamines not recommended for chronic use.

treatments.name: Trial of NSAID Avoidance
treatments.result: Counseled to avoid ibuprofen and try acetaminophen instead. NSAIDs can worsen CSU.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs worsen urticaria in ~30% of CSU patients. Trial of avoidance reasonable.

treatments.name: Discuss Omalizumab if Antihistamines Fail
treatments.result: Counseled that if high-dose antihistamines fail after 2-4 weeks, omalizumab (Xolair) is next step. 300mg SQ monthly. Highly effective for CSU.
treatments.reasoning: Omalizumab is guideline-recommended for antihistamine-refractory CSU. 65-90% response rate.

treatments.name: Steroid Counseling
treatments.result: Counseled that while steroids help short-term, they are NOT appropriate for chronic management due to side effects. May use short bursts for severe flares only.
treatments.reasoning: Important to set expectations - patients often request steroids after good response.

disposition.name: Follow-up in 4 Weeks
disposition.result: Return in 4 weeks to assess response to high-dose antihistamines. If inadequate control, will initiate omalizumab.
disposition.reasoning: Early follow-up to assess response and escalate if needed.

disposition.name: Track Symptoms with UAS7
disposition.result: Patient instructed to track daily itch and hive scores (UAS7). Helps quantify disease activity and treatment response.
disposition.reasoning: UAS7 is validated tool for monitoring CSU - useful for tracking treatment efficacy.

disposition.name: Patient Education
disposition.result: Educated that CSU is common, not caused by allergy, often associated with autoimmunity, and usually resolves over months to years. Treatments are effective.
disposition.reasoning: Patient understanding reduces anxiety and prevents unnecessary avoidance behaviors.

actualDiagnosis.name: Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria with Autoimmune Thyroid Association
teachingPoints: Chronic urticaria = hives ≥6 weeks duration
CSU (no trigger) is most common form - NOT caused by food or environmental allergies
Limited workup: CBC, ESR/CRP, TSH, anti-TPO. Extensive testing NOT recommended.
Treatment ladder: Second-gen antihistamine → Uptitrate to 4x dose → Omalizumab
AVOID first-generation antihistamines and chronic systemic steroids
30% of CSU patients have thyroid autoantibodies - check even if TSH normal
Most CSU resolves within 1-5 years even without treatment

female-35-hives.teachingPoint: CSU is NOT an allergic disease and allergy testing is NOT recommended. The key teaching points: (1) Limited workup - don't over-test, (2) Uptitrate antihistamines to 4x before adding other therapies, (3) Check thyroid antibodies even with normal TSH, (4) Omalizumab is highly effective for refractory cases, (5) Avoid chronic steroids.
female-35-hives.clinicalPearl: The '24-hour rule' for urticaria: If individual hives last MORE than 24 hours and leave bruising, consider urticarial vasculitis and biopsy. If hives last LESS than 24 hours and fade completely, it's typical urticaria and biopsy is not needed.

options.text: No additional testing - low IgG confirms CVID

options.text: Check vaccine responses (pneumococcal, tetanus) and exclude secondary causes
options.explanation: Correct! CVID requires both low immunoglobulins AND poor vaccine responses. Must exclude secondary causes (HIV, medications, protein loss, malignancy).

options.text: Genetic testing is required for diagnosis

options.explanation: Inadequate - patients need therapeutic levels to prevent infections.

options.text: Greater than 500 mg/dL, higher if still having infections

female-32-cvid.oldcartsNarrative:
"I've always been the one who catches every cold, but the last few years have been ridiculous. I've had probably 6-7 sinus infections a year that need antibiotics. I've had pneumonia three times in the last two years - twice I was hospitalized. 

My primary care doctor finally did some blood tests and said my immunoglobulins are low. She referred me here. I've never heard of immunodeficiency before - I thought that was something you were born with or got from HIV.

I'm tired of being sick all the time. I work as an accountant and I've used up all my sick days. My husband is frustrated because we can't plan anything without me getting sick. I had to cancel our honeymoon trip because I had pneumonia.

Looking back, I think I was always sicker than other kids. I had my tonsils and adenoids out when I was 8 because of chronic infections. I had a lot of ear infections too. But no one ever mentioned immunodeficiency.

I don't have HIV - I was tested when I got married. I don't take any medications that would affect my immune system. I'm really hoping there's something that can help."

historyQuestions.text: How often do you get infections?
historyQuestions.answer: Sinus infections every 6-8 weeks it seems. Pneumonia three times in two years. I feel like I'm always on antibiotics.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections are hallmark of antibody deficiency.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the pneumonias.
historyQuestions.answer: First one was two years ago - I was in the hospital for 4 days on IV antibiotics. Second one a year ago, hospitalized again. Third one 6 months ago I managed at home with oral antibiotics but it took 3 weeks to get better.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple pneumonias requiring hospitalization strongly suggests immunodeficiency.

historyQuestions.text: Do you know what organisms caused your infections?
historyQuestions.answer: They said one pneumonia was Streptococcus pneumoniae. The sinus infections they usually don't culture - they just give me antibiotics.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) are characteristic of antibody deficiency.

historyQuestions.text: How do you respond to antibiotics?
historyQuestions.answer: They usually work but it takes longer than it should. My husband gets over sinus infections in a week - mine take 2-3 weeks. Sometimes I need a second course.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged infections and need for extended/repeated antibiotics suggests immune dysfunction.

historyQuestions.text: Any other types of infections - skin, GI, urinary?
historyQuestions.answer: I get diarrhea a lot actually. My doctor thought it was IBS but it comes and goes. No skin infections really. UTIs occasionally but not more than other women I think.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI symptoms common in CVID - can be infectious (Giardia) or inflammatory (CVID enteropathy).

historyQuestions.text: Were you sick a lot as a child?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, definitely. Lots of ear infections, sore throats, sinus stuff. I had my tonsils and adenoids out at 8. My mom said I was always at the doctor.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Childhood history of recurrent infections suggests lifelong immune dysfunction - though CVID often isn't diagnosed until adulthood.

historyQuestions.text: Any hospitalizations as a child?
historyQuestions.answer: Once for a bad ear infection that spread - they called it mastoiditis. I was maybe 6 or 7.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Serious childhood infections (mastoiditis) add to pattern of immune dysfunction.

historyQuestions.text: Are you up to date on vaccinations?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I get my flu shot every year. I had the pneumonia vaccine - both kinds actually. Still got pneumonia though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Getting pneumonia despite pneumococcal vaccination suggests failure to make protective antibodies - key diagnostic feature of CVID.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been tested for HIV?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, when I got married and again after the first pneumonia. Negative both times.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must exclude HIV as cause of secondary immunodeficiency.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just a multivitamin and vitamin D. Whatever antibiotic I'm on at the moment. Birth control pills.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No immunosuppressive medications. Rituximab, chronic steroids, certain chemotherapies can cause secondary hypogammaglobulinemia.

historyQuestions.text: Any problems with protein in your urine or chronic diarrhea causing weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: The diarrhea comes and goes but I haven't lost weight. No one's mentioned protein in my urine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Protein-losing enteropathy and nephrotic syndrome can cause hypogammaglobulinemia.

historyQuestions.text: Any unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or lumps?
historyQuestions.answer: No weight loss. No lumps that I've noticed. I do sometimes have night sweats when I'm sick but not otherwise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hematologic malignancy (CLL, myeloma) can cause secondary hypogammaglobulinemia.

historyQuestions.text: Does anyone in your family have immune problems or get a lot of infections?
historyQuestions.answer: My brother was always sick as a kid too. He still gets bronchitis a lot. No one's been diagnosed with immunodeficiency though. My cousin died of lymphoma at 45.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of infections and lymphoma supports inherited component. CVID has familial clustering.

historyQuestions.text: Any joint pain, rashes, or other autoimmune symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: My joints ache sometimes when I'm sick but nothing diagnosed. No rashes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Autoimmunity occurs in 20-30% of CVID patients - ITP, AIHA, autoimmune thyroid disease.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: Just the chronic sinus stuff. They did a CT scan once and said I have some scarring in my sinuses.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic sinus disease with scarring from repeated infections.

historyQuestions.text: Besides tonsils and adenoids, any other surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my appendix out in college. And sinus surgery once to open up my sinuses - it helped for a while.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history - sinus surgery reflects severity of sinus disease.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies to medications.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment planning.

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an accountant. Office job, not a lot of exposure to sick people except during tax season when everyone is stressed and sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupation and exposure risk.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Never smoked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking would worsen lung disease.

historyQuestions.text: Do you drink alcohol?
historyQuestions.answer: Rarely - maybe a glass of wine on special occasions. I feel like alcohol makes me more likely to get sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol use for general health assessment.

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your life?
historyQuestions.answer: Significantly. I've used all my sick days at work. We had to cancel our honeymoon. My husband and I want to start a family but I'm scared of being pregnant and this sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Quality of life impact - pregnancy planning important in CVID.

physicalExam.finding: BP 116/72, HR 76, RR 16, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 97%. Vitals normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals - not acutely ill today.

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing woman, slightly pale. No acute distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Appears well between infections.

physicalExam.finding: Tonsils absent (prior tonsillectomy). Nasal mucosa edematous with purulent discharge. Post-nasal drip visible. TMs clear.
physicalExam.reasoning: Evidence of chronic sinus disease. Absent tonsils from childhood surgery.

physicalExam.finding: Small anterior cervical lymph nodes palpable bilaterally. No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No splenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lymphadenopathy can occur in CVID. Splenomegaly may be present. No hepatosplenomegaly here.

physicalExam.finding: Scattered crackles at bilateral bases. No wheezing. Cough productive of yellow sputum.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bibasilar crackles concerning for bronchiectasis from recurrent pneumonias.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Baseline cardiac exam.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Splenomegaly can occur in CVID. Normal here.

physicalExam.finding: No rashes. No granulomas. Skin intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Granulomatous disease occurs in subset of CVID. Not present here.

labs.name: Quantitative Immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM)
labs.result: IgG 285 mg/dL (LOW, normal 700-1600), IgA 18 mg/dL (LOW, normal 70-400), IgM 32 mg/dL (LOW, normal 40-230).
labs.reasoning: Panhypogammaglobulinemia confirms antibody deficiency. Low IgG + low IgA and/or IgM is required for CVID diagnosis.

labs.name: Vaccine Antibody Titers (Pneumococcal, Tetanus, Diphtheria)
labs.result: Pneumococcal titers: Protective (>1.3 mcg/mL) to only 2 of 23 serotypes despite vaccination. Tetanus: 0.08 IU/mL (borderline). Diphtheria: 0.05 IU/mL (non-protective).
labs.reasoning: Poor vaccine responses confirm antibody dysfunction. This is a required diagnostic criterion for CVID.

labs.name: CBC with Differential
labs.result: WBC 5.8, Hgb 11.2 (mild anemia), Plt 195. Lymphocytes 1.2 (low-normal).
labs.reasoning: Mild anemia common in CVID. Lymphopenia can occur. Check for cytopenias suggesting autoimmunity.

labs.name: Lymphocyte Subsets by Flow Cytometry
labs.result: CD19+ B cells: 8% (normal 5-20%). CD4+ T cells: 680/μL (normal). CD8+ T cells: 420/μL (normal). T cells present and normal.
labs.reasoning: B cells present (not agammaglobulinemia). T cell numbers normal (not combined immunodeficiency).

labs.name: HIV 1/2 Antibody/Antigen
labs.result: Negative.
labs.reasoning: Must exclude HIV as cause of secondary immunodeficiency.

labs.name: Serum Protein Electrophoresis
labs.result: Decreased gamma globulin fraction. No monoclonal protein.
labs.reasoning: Rules out myeloma. Confirms hypogammaglobulinemia.

labs.name: Urinalysis with Protein
labs.result: No proteinuria.
labs.reasoning: Rules out nephrotic syndrome as cause of immunoglobulin loss.

imaging.name: CT Chest (High Resolution)
imaging.result: Bilateral lower lobe bronchiectasis with scattered tree-in-bud opacities. No masses. Mild mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
imaging.reasoning: Bronchiectasis confirms lung damage from recurrent infections. Important baseline before starting treatment.

imaging.name: CT Sinuses
imaging.result: Chronic pan-sinusitis with mucosal thickening. Prior surgery changes. No acute air-fluid levels.

treatments.name: Start Immunoglobulin Replacement (IVIG or SCIG)
treatments.result: Initiated IVIG 400 mg/kg every 4 weeks. Pre-medicated with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine. First infusion tolerated well.
treatments.reasoning: Immunoglobulin replacement is the mainstay of CVID treatment. Reduces infection frequency and prevents further lung damage.

treatments.name: Consider Prophylactic Antibiotics
treatments.result: Started azithromycin 250mg three times weekly for pulmonary bronchiectasis prophylaxis.
treatments.reasoning: Chronic macrolide can reduce exacerbations in bronchiectasis.

treatments.name: Pulmonary Rehabilitation/Airway Clearance
treatments.result: Referred to pulmonary rehabilitation for airway clearance techniques given bronchiectasis.
treatments.reasoning: Airway clearance helps prevent mucus stasis and infection in bronchiectasis.

consults.name: Pulmonology Referral
consults.result: Referred for bronchiectasis management and monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Co-management of bronchiectasis important for CVID patients.

consults.name: Consider Genetic Testing
consults.result: Offered genetic testing for monogenic causes of CVID (ICOS, CD19, etc.). Patient considering.
consults.reasoning: Genetic testing can identify specific mutations in ~25% of CVID but doesn't change treatment.

disposition.name: Follow-up Schedule
disposition.result: Monthly for first 3 months for IVIG and trough monitoring. Check IgG trough before each infusion. Adjust dose to target trough >500 mg/dL (higher if still having infections).
disposition.reasoning: Close monitoring needed when initiating therapy. Dose adjustments based on trough levels and clinical response.

disposition.name: Long-term Monitoring Plan
disposition.result: Annual: CT chest for bronchiectasis progression, CBC (cytopenias), LFTs (liver disease), consider colonoscopy surveillance (increased GI malignancy risk).
disposition.reasoning: CVID has complications requiring surveillance: bronchiectasis, cytopenias, liver disease, malignancy.

disposition.name: Vaccination Guidance
disposition.result: Counseled: Live vaccines contraindicated. Inactivated vaccines may not be effective but are safe. Household members should be vaccinated.
disposition.reasoning: Live vaccines risky; killed vaccines safe but may not produce response. Cocooning strategy important.

options.explanation: The combination is too specific for coincidence.

male-45-eosinophilia.oldcartsNarrative:
"My primary care doctor did routine blood work and found my eosinophils are high - I think she said 2,500? She seemed concerned and referred me here. I feel mostly fine but now I'm worried.

I do have allergies and asthma - I've had them for years. I use an inhaler when I need it, maybe a few times a week. My allergies are bad in the spring but otherwise manageable. I take Zyrtec.

The thing that's been bothering me lately is some numbness and tingling in my feet. Started maybe 2-3 months ago. My right foot especially - feels like it's falling asleep but doesn't wake up. I thought it was from sitting too much at my desk.

I also have had sinus problems for years - chronic congestion, postnasal drip. I've had a few sinus infections but nothing unusual.

I traveled to Southeast Asia about 6 months ago for work - spent 2 weeks in Vietnam. I was careful about what I ate but you never know. No diarrhea or anything though.

I'm an engineer, work at a desk. Don't smoke. Drink occasionally. I take lisinopril for blood pressure and atorvastatin for cholesterol. And the Zyrtec."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your allergies.
historyQuestions.answer: I've had hay fever since I was a teenager - sneezing, runny nose, itchy eyes in spring. I'm allergic to tree pollen and dust mites. It's controlled with Zyrtec usually.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Allergic rhinitis is common cause of mild eosinophilia but rarely causes AEC >1500.

historyQuestions.text: How is your asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: I've had it for about 10 years. It's been getting worse the last year or so - I use my rescue inhaler maybe 4-5 times a week now. I wake up coughing sometimes. I'm on Flovent but maybe I need something stronger.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Late-onset asthma that's worsening, combined with eosinophilia and neuropathy, is concerning for EGPA.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the numbness in your feet.
historyQuestions.answer: It started about 2-3 months ago. My right foot feels numb and tingly, like pins and needles. Sometimes my left foot too. Recently I've noticed my right hand feels a bit weak when I grip things.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 🚨 RED FLAG: Mononeuritis multiplex pattern (asymmetric sensorimotor neuropathy) is classic for EGPA vasculitis.

historyQuestions.text: Is the numbness getting worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, it's definitely spreading. First just my right foot, now both feet and starting in my hands.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive mononeuritis multiplex - urgent concern for vasculitic neuropathy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your sinus problems.
historyQuestions.answer: Chronic congestion for years. Thick mucus, postnasal drip, decreased smell. I've had several sinus infections that needed antibiotics. A CT scan once showed my sinuses were clouded up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic sinusitis with nasal polyposis is part of EGPA criteria.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your travel to Southeast Asia.
historyQuestions.answer: I was in Vietnam for 2 weeks about 6 months ago for work. Stayed in hotels in cities mostly. I was careful about water - drank bottled only. Ate at restaurants, some street food. No diarrhea or illness during or after.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SE Asia travel - risk for strongyloides, hookworm, other parasites. Important to screen.

historyQuestions.text: Any other travel or places you've lived?
historyQuestions.answer: Born and raised in California. Traveled to Mexico once years ago. Work trips to Asia occasionally.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Travel history for parasitic exposures.

historyQuestions.text: Any stomach or intestinal symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really. My appetite is fine. No diarrhea. Sometimes a little bloating after meals but nothing major.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for EoE, eosinophilic gastroenteritis, parasitic GI involvement.

historyQuestions.text: Any trouble swallowing or food getting stuck?
historyQuestions.answer: No, swallowing is fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dysphagia would suggest EoE.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Lisinopril for blood pressure, atorvastatin for cholesterol, cetirizine for allergies, and Flovent inhaler for asthma. Sometimes ibuprofen for headaches.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Drug review for eosinophilia causes. NSAIDs can cause eosinophilia but lisinopril more commonly associated.

historyQuestions.text: Any new medications in the last 6 months?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been on the same medications for a few years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No new drugs - less likely drug-induced.

historyQuestions.text: Any rashes or skin problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I get eczema sometimes on my hands, especially in winter. Nothing new recently.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Skin manifestations of EGPA include palpable purpura, nodules. Eczema is atopic.

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers, weight loss, or night sweats?
historyQuestions.answer: No fevers. My weight has been stable. No night sweats.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constitutional symptoms would suggest systemic disease or malignancy.

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain, shortness of breath, or swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: The asthma causes some shortness of breath but no chest pain. No leg swelling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiac symptoms would suggest eosinophilic heart disease - critical to screen.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: Just the high blood pressure and high cholesterol. And the allergies and asthma.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline medical history.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of autoimmune diseases or blood disorders?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother has rheumatoid arthritis. No blood cancers or anything like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family autoimmune history noted.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a beer or two on weekends. Nothing heavy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social history.

procedures.result: Axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy with mononeuritis multiplex pattern. Right peroneal and left sural nerves most affected.

treatments.name: High-Dose Corticosteroids

treatments.reasoning: Corticosteroids are first-line for EGPA. High-dose needed for organ involvement.

treatments.name: Consider Mepolizumab (after induction)

treatments.reasoning: Mepolizumab reduces eosinophils and is FDA-approved for EGPA maintenance.

disposition.reasoning: Close monitoring during treatment initiation and taper.

disposition.name: Long-term Monitoring
disposition.result: Monitor for relapse (rising eosinophils, worsening symptoms). Annual echo. Watch for steroid side effects.
disposition.reasoning: EGPA can relapse - ongoing surveillance needed.

actualDiagnosis.name: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA/Churg-Strauss)
teachingPoints: EGPA diagnostic criteria: Asthma + eosinophilia >1500 + 2 of: biopsy-proven vasculitis, neuropathy, pulmonary infiltrates, sinonasal disease, extravascular eosinophils
ANCA is positive in 40-60% (MPO/p-ANCA pattern typical)
Three phases: allergic (asthma, rhinitis), eosinophilic (infiltrates), vasculitic (nerve, heart, skin)
Must screen heart with echo - cardiac involvement is leading cause of death
Treatment: corticosteroids for all; add cyclophosphamide or rituximab for severe disease
Five-Factor Score predicts mortality: cardiac involvement, GI involvement, renal insufficiency, proteinuria, CNS involvement
Mepolizumab (anti-IL5) FDA-approved for maintenance

revisedDifferential.name: Simple Allergic Disease
keyFeatures: Doesn't explain neuropathy or this degree of eosinophilia

male-45-eosinophilia.teachingPoint: The combination of asthma + eosinophilia + mononeuritis multiplex should immediately trigger consideration of EGPA. This is one of the classic presentations. The neuropathy indicates vasculitic phase and requires aggressive immunosuppression beyond steroids alone.
male-45-eosinophilia.clinicalPearl: Remember 'EGPA finds its way to the heart' - cardiac involvement occurs in up to 60% and is the leading cause of death. Always get an echocardiogram in hypereosinophilia, even without cardiac symptoms. Endomyocardial fibrosis can be silent until advanced.

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination

Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

keyPoints: Systemic Mastocytosis: Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL, KIT D816V mutation, abnormal mast cells on biopsy

male-52-mast-cell.chiefComplaint: I keep having episodes where I flush bright red, feel like I'm going to pass out, and get terrible diarrhea. My doctor found my tryptase is high.

missedConsequences.Systemic Mastocytosis: Without diagnosis, severe anaphylaxis risk especially with Hymenoptera stings, surgery, contrast. Can progress to aggressive disease.

missedConsequences.Carcinoid Syndrome: Missed neuroendocrine tumor with flushing, diarrhea - requires different treatment.
missedConsequences.Pheochromocytoma: Missed diagnosis could result in hypertensive crisis during surgery.

Confusing MCAS with mastocytosis - different diagnostic criteria
Not doing bone marrow biopsy when tryptase elevated

Not counseling about anesthesia/surgery risks

options.explanation: Correct! Mastocytosis patients need premedication protocol, trigger avoidance (certain anesthetics, opioids, NSAIDs), and immediate access to epinephrine.

male-52-mast-cell.oldcartsNarrative:
"These episodes started about 2 years ago. Out of nowhere, I'll suddenly feel hot, my face and chest turn bright red, and I feel like the room is spinning. My heart races and I feel like I might pass out. A few times I've actually had to sit down on the floor so I wouldn't fall. Then I get terrible stomach cramps and diarrhea.

The episodes last maybe 30-60 minutes and then gradually get better. They happen maybe 2-3 times a month. I can't figure out what triggers them - sometimes it's after drinking alcohol, sometimes after a hot shower, sometimes for no reason at all.

Here's the scary part - about a year ago I got stung by a bee and had a terrible reaction. My whole body broke out in hives, my throat felt tight, I got dizzy and almost passed out. The paramedics gave me an epinephrine shot and took me to the ER. The ER doctor did a blood test and said my 'tryptase' was really high - like 45 or something. They told me to follow up but I didn't understand why it was important.

My regular doctor just checked my tryptase again and it's still elevated - 28 I think he said. He also noticed some spots on my chest that he called 'suspicious' and sent me here.

I work in construction so I'm outside a lot - I'm terrified of getting stung again. I carry the EpiPen they gave me everywhere. My wife is worried sick about me."

historyQuestions.text: Describe a typical episode.
historyQuestions.answer: It starts with suddenly feeling hot and flushed. My face and chest turn bright red - my wife says I look like a lobster. My heart pounds, I feel dizzy like I might faint. Then my stomach cramps up badly and I have to rush to the bathroom with diarrhea. The whole thing lasts 30-60 minutes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic mast cell mediator release symptoms: flushing, tachycardia, hypotension, GI symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: How often do these episodes happen?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 2-3 times a month. Sometimes more if I'm not careful about triggers.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recurrent episodic symptoms suggest ongoing mast cell activation.

historyQuestions.text: Have you identified any triggers?
historyQuestions.answer: Alcohol definitely - even one beer can set it off. Hot showers. Stress sometimes. Exercise occasionally. NSAIDs seem to make things worse. And obviously the bee sting was the worst.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic mast cell degranulation triggers: alcohol, heat, stress, NSAIDs, Hymenoptera venom.

historyQuestions.text: Have you actually passed out?
historyQuestions.answer: Almost - I've had to sit or lie down several times because I felt like I would. Once I did lose consciousness briefly at home - my wife caught me. That's when she made me go to the doctor.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Near-syncope/syncope from hypotension during mast cell degranulation - serious manifestation.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the bee sting reaction.
historyQuestions.answer: It was about a year ago. I was working on a job site and got stung on my arm. Within minutes I was covered in hives, my throat felt like it was closing, I was dizzy and nauseous. A coworker called 911. The paramedics gave me epinephrine and I felt better. In the ER they said my tryptase was really high.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe anaphylaxis to Hymenoptera with elevated tryptase is highly suggestive of mastocytosis - 5-7% of severe Hymenoptera anaphylaxis have underlying mastocytosis.

historyQuestions.text: Had you been stung before without problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I've been stung maybe 4-5 times over the years working construction. Never had a problem before - just local swelling. This was completely different.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior tolerance with new severe reaction pattern - sensitization plus underlying mast cell disease.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the spots your doctor noticed.
historyQuestions.answer: I've had these brownish spots on my chest and back for years - I thought they were just age spots or freckles. But my doctor pressed on one and it got red and itchy and raised up. He seemed concerned.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 🚨 URTICARIA PIGMENTOSA with positive Darier sign (urtication with stroking) - pathognomonic for cutaneous mastocytosis.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any GI symptoms between episodes?
historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes - I've had chronic loose stools for a few years. Some bloating. I was told I have IBS but nothing really helps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic GI symptoms (diarrhea, bloating) from mast cell infiltration of GI tract - common in systemic mastocytosis.

historyQuestions.text: Any bone pain or fractures?
historyQuestions.answer: My lower back has been aching for a couple years. I figured it was from construction work. No fractures though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bone pain can indicate mast cell infiltration. Osteoporosis/osteopenia common in mastocytosis - need DEXA.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just over-the-counter stuff - ibuprofen for aches, sometimes Pepto for my stomach. I have the EpiPen but haven't needed it since the bee sting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAIDs can trigger mast cell degranulation - need to avoid. No preventive mast cell medications yet.

historyQuestions.text: Have you tried antihistamines?
historyQuestions.answer: I take Zyrtec occasionally for allergies but never consistently. It might help a little with the flushing.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Antihistamines are first-line therapy - needs consistent H1 and H2 blockade.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure - I take lisinopril. That's about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medical history. ACE inhibitors can potentially affect mast cell reactions.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: Not that I know of, but after all this I'm nervous about taking anything new.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment planning.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of similar problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nobody else has these symptoms. My mom has some skin spots but never had the other stuff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mastocytosis is usually sporadic, not inherited. Familial mastocytosis rare.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work.
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a foreman at a construction company. Outdoor work mostly. That's why I'm so worried about bees - I'm outside all summer.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupational Hymenoptera exposure risk - makes venom immunotherapy more urgent.

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I used to have a couple beers after work but I've pretty much stopped because it triggers episodes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient already avoiding alcohol trigger - good insight.

historyQuestions.text: Do you smoke?
historyQuestions.answer: Quit 10 years ago. Smoked a pack a day for 20 years before that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Smoking history.

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your life?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly, I'm scared all the time. I'm afraid to eat certain foods, drink anything, get too hot, exercise. My wife and I don't go out much anymore. I feel like I'm walking on eggshells waiting for the next episode.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant quality of life impact - treatment urgency.

procedures.name: Bone Marrow Biopsy with Special Stains
procedures.result: Multifocal dense aggregates of mast cells (>15 cells per aggregate). Mast cells show abnormal spindle morphology. Immunohistochemistry: CD117+, CD25+, CD2+. KIT D816V mutation confirmed. Consistent with SYSTEMIC MASTOCYTOSIS - Indolent subtype (ISM).
procedures.reasoning: GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis. Shows major criterion (dense mast cell aggregates) and minor criteria (abnormal morphology, CD25+, KIT mutation).

procedures.name: Skin Biopsy (of pigmented lesion)
procedures.result: Increased mast cells in dermis, consistent with urticaria pigmentosa/cutaneous mastocytosis.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms cutaneous involvement but bone marrow is more important for systemic disease.

treatments.result: Started cetirizine 10mg twice daily for continuous H1 blockade.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment - H1 blocker for flushing, pruritus, urticaria.

treatments.name: H2-Antihistamine (Famotidine 20mg BID)
treatments.result: Added famotidine 20mg twice daily for GI symptoms and additional mast cell stabilization.
treatments.reasoning: H2 blockers help GI symptoms and provide additional mast cell mediator blockade.

treatments.name: Cromolyn Sodium (Oral)
treatments.result: Started cromolyn 200mg QID before meals for GI symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: Mast cell stabilizer - particularly helpful for GI symptoms.

treatments.name: Epinephrine Auto-injectors (x2)
treatments.result: Confirmed patient has EpiPen. Prescribed second device. Reviewed proper use and when to use.
treatments.reasoning: All mastocytosis patients need epinephrine for anaphylaxis risk.

treatments.name: Trigger Avoidance Counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on avoiding triggers: alcohol, NSAIDs, extreme temperatures, opioids, certain anesthetics. Provided list of safer alternatives.
treatments.reasoning: Trigger avoidance is key to reducing episodes.

treatments.name: Venom Immunotherapy
treatments.result: Initiated venom immunotherapy to honeybee. Will continue lifelong given mastocytosis (normally 3-5 years). Provides significant protection against future sting reactions.
treatments.reasoning: VIT is indicated for mastocytosis patients with Hymenoptera anaphylaxis. Must continue indefinitely (lifelong) unlike typical VIT.

treatments.name: Calcium/Vitamin D, Consider Bisphosphonate
treatments.result: Started calcium 1200mg + vitamin D 2000IU daily. Discussed bisphosphonate for osteopenia - patient prefers monitoring for now.
treatments.reasoning: Bone protection important given osteopenia from mastocytosis.

treatments.name: Surgical Precautions Letter
treatments.result: Provided letter for any future procedures documenting mastocytosis diagnosis, premedication protocol (H1, H2, steroids), trigger avoidance (avoid morphine, prefer fentanyl; avoid certain muscle relaxants), and need for epinephrine availability.
treatments.reasoning: Critical safety measure - perioperative anaphylaxis risk.

consults.name: Hematology Referral
consults.result: Referred for co-management of systemic mastocytosis. Will monitor for disease progression.
consults.reasoning: Mastocytosis is a hematologic/oncologic condition - needs specialist follow-up.

disposition.name: Follow-up Plan
disposition.result: Return in 4 weeks to assess medication response. Annual tryptase, CBC, CMP to monitor for progression. Annual DEXA. Venom immunotherapy maintenance.
disposition.reasoning: Regular monitoring for disease progression and treatment response.

actualDiagnosis.name: Systemic Mastocytosis - Indolent Subtype (ISM)
keyFindings: Bone marrow showing mast cell aggregates
teachingPoints: WHO criteria for systemic mastocytosis: 1 major + 1 minor OR 3 minor criteria
Major criterion: Multifocal dense mast cell aggregates (≥15 cells) in bone marrow or extracutaneous organ
Minor criteria: (1) >25% spindle-shaped mast cells, (2) KIT D816V mutation, (3) CD25 and/or CD2 expression, (4) Tryptase >20 ng/mL
Always check tryptase in severe Hymenoptera anaphylaxis - 5-7% have mastocytosis
Indolent SM has good prognosis - treatment is symptomatic
H1 + H2 antihistamines + cromolyn are first-line
VIT is lifelong in mastocytosis (not 3-5 years)
Perioperative precautions essential - provide letter for any procedures

male-52-mast-cell.teachingPoint: The triad of urticaria pigmentosa + elevated baseline tryptase + severe Hymenoptera anaphylaxis is essentially diagnostic of systemic mastocytosis pending bone marrow confirmation. Always check baseline tryptase in severe insect sting reactions.
male-52-mast-cell.clinicalPearl: The Darier sign (urtication when stroking a pigmented lesion) is pathognomonic for mastocytosis. If you see suspicious pigmented macules on the trunk, stroke one firmly and watch for whealing - this simple bedside test can make the diagnosis.
