mcqs.question: An 8-year-old had hives and vomiting within 30 minutes of eating peanut butter. Peanut skin test is 12mm wheal. What is the MOST accurate statement?

options.text: The large skin test confirms peanut allergy
options.explanation: Skin test shows sensitization, not clinical allergy. However, combined with convincing history, diagnosis is likely.

options.text: The clinical history of immediate reaction plus positive skin test strongly supports IgE-mediated peanut allergy
options.explanation: Correct! The combination of convincing history (immediate symptoms after ingestion) plus positive testing = high probability of true allergy. Oral food challenge could confirm but is often not needed with clear history.

options.text: An oral food challenge is required before diagnosis
options.explanation: With clear history and positive testing, OFC is not required and would be risky.

options.text: This is likely coincidental and peanut can be reintroduced
options.explanation: Immediate reaction with hives and vomiting after peanut exposure is highly consistent with IgE-mediated allergy.

mcqs.question: The child is confirmed to have peanut allergy. The parents ask if they should also avoid all tree nuts. What is the best response?

options.text: Yes, always avoid all tree nuts with peanut allergy
options.explanation: Peanut is a legume, not a tree nut. Cross-reactivity is not automatic.

options.text: Test for tree nut sensitization; avoid only those with positive tests and/or reactions
options.explanation: Correct! Peanut allergic patients don't automatically need to avoid tree nuts. Test individually and make decisions based on sensitization, history, and family preference. Many can tolerate tree nuts.

options.text: Tree nuts are safe since peanut is a legume
options.explanation: Not necessarily safe - co-allergy to tree nuts occurs in ~30% of peanut allergic patients. Testing is needed.

options.text: Only avoid tree nuts if they've had a reaction
options.explanation: Should test for sensitization - some may be sensitized and at risk for reaction on first known exposure.

allDiagnoses.name: IgE-Mediated Peanut Allergy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Immediate onset hives and vomiting after peanut ingestion is classic IgE-mediated food allergy.

allDiagnoses.name: Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis (FPIES)
allDiagnoses.rationale: FPIES causes delayed vomiting (2-4 hours) without hives. This was immediate with urticaria.

allDiagnoses.name: Oral Allergy Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: OAS causes localized oral symptoms, not systemic hives and vomiting.

allDiagnoses.name: Coincidental Viral Illness
allDiagnoses.rationale: Timing immediately after peanut ingestion makes coincidence unlikely.

child-8-peanut.oldcartsNarrative:
(Parents speaking): "We're here because Ethan had a scary reaction about 3 weeks ago. He was at a birthday party and ate a brownie that apparently had peanut butter in it - we didn't know. Within about 20-30 minutes, he started itching and broke out in hives all over his body. Then he started throwing up. We gave him Benadryl and it got better over the next hour or so. We didn't go to the ER but we were really scared.

He's never eaten much peanut butter - we didn't give it to him as a baby because his older sister has eczema and we were worried. He might have had a little bit at preschool but we're not sure. This is the first time we know for sure he ate peanut and reacted.

He has eczema - it was really bad as a baby but is better now. He also seems to get a runny nose in spring. His sister has eczema too and is allergic to eggs, which she's grown out of mostly.

We need to know for sure if he's allergic because he's in school and we need to tell them what to do. We're also wondering about tree nuts - should he avoid everything? His favorite snack is cashews and we're scared to give them to him now.

We want to know all our options. We've heard there are treatments now for peanut allergy?"

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly what happened when Ethan ate the peanut.
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) He ate a brownie at a birthday party - we found out later it had peanut butter. Maybe 20-30 minutes later he started scratching and we saw hives popping up on his face, arms, stomach. Then he threw up twice. His lips looked a little puffy. He didn't have trouble breathing but he seemed scared. We gave him Benadryl and watched him - it got better over about an hour.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic IgE-mediated reaction: rapid onset (minutes), urticaria, angioedema, GI symptoms. Concerning for possible anaphylaxis.

historyQuestions.text: Any breathing problems, coughing, or wheezing?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) He didn't seem to have trouble breathing but he was crying a lot so it was hard to tell. No wheezing that we heard. His voice sounded normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No clear respiratory symptoms this time, but GI symptoms + urticaria + angioedema meets criteria for anaphylaxis (multi-system involvement).

historyQuestions.text: How much of the brownie did he eat?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) Most of it - it was a regular-sized brownie. He said it tasted really good, probably because of the peanut butter.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Full serving - significant exposure. Some patients react to traces.

historyQuestions.text: Had he ever eaten peanut before?
historyQuestions.answer: (Dad) We're not 100% sure. We didn't give him peanut butter as a baby because of the eczema and his sister's allergies. At preschool they had 'peanut-free' snacks so probably not there. This might have been his first real exposure, or maybe he had a tiny bit somewhere and we didn't notice.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Limited prior exposure - consistent with having developed sensitization without tolerance from lack of early introduction.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about his eczema.
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) He had really bad eczema as a baby - face, arms, legs, trunk. We used steroid creams and moisturizers constantly. It's better now - just some dry patches in the winter, mainly in his elbow and knee creases.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant eczema history is a major risk factor for food allergy. Impaired skin barrier allows food sensitization.

historyQuestions.text: Any other food allergies or reactions?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) Not that we know of. He eats eggs, milk, wheat, everything else without problems. We've been avoiding tree nuts since this happened just to be safe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tolerates other common allergens. Tree nuts need to be tested - not automatic avoidance.

historyQuestions.text: Does he have seasonal allergies or asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: (Dad) He gets a runny nose and sneezes in the spring - we give him Zyrtec and it helps. No asthma - he's never had wheezing or needed an inhaler.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Allergic rhinitis confirms atopic background. No asthma is reassuring (asthma increases anaphylaxis severity risk).

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of food allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) His older sister had an egg allergy - she's mostly outgrown it now. I have hay fever and my husband has eczema. My nephew has peanut allergy too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Strong family history of atopy and food allergy - increases Ethan's risk.

historyQuestions.text: Has he ever eaten tree nuts like cashews, almonds, walnuts?
historyQuestions.answer: (Dad) Yes, cashews are actually his favorite snack! He's eaten almonds in granola bars too. Never had a problem with those. We stopped giving them after the peanut reaction because we didn't know what to do.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tolerates tree nuts clinically - important! Peanut is a legume, not tree nut. Can likely continue tree nuts if testing supports.

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) No, he's a healthy kid otherwise. Just the eczema and spring allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Otherwise healthy - no comorbidities affecting management.

historyQuestions.text: What medications does he take?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) Zyrtec in the spring for allergies. We use moisturizer and occasional hydrocortisone for the eczema. We had liquid Benadryl at home which is what we gave him during the reaction.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Current medications - relevant for skin testing (antihistamines).

historyQuestions.text: What are your biggest concerns?
historyQuestions.answer: (Dad) School is the big one. He's in second grade and we need to make sure he's safe at lunch and snack time. We also need to know about birthday parties, playdates - it's overwhelming. And we want to know if there's anything we can do to make him less allergic - we've heard about treatments.
historyQuestions.reasoning: School safety is critical - need action plan and 504. Family interested in treatment options (OIT).

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned hearing about treatments?
historyQuestions.answer: (Mom) We read about peanut allergy treatment where they give small amounts of peanut to build tolerance. We want to know if Ethan is a candidate for that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Interest in oral immunotherapy - will discuss options.

physicalExam.finding: BP 98/62, HR 88, RR 18, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 99%. Normal pediatric vitals.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals today - not having reaction.

physicalExam.finding: Well-appearing 8-year-old boy, age-appropriate development. Active and interactive.
physicalExam.reasoning: Appears healthy.

physicalExam.finding: Mild xerosis (dry skin). Lichenified patches in antecubital and popliteal fossae consistent with eczema. No active urticaria or angioedema today.
physicalExam.reasoning: Chronic eczema findings - confirms atopic diathesis. No acute allergic findings today.

physicalExam.finding: Pale, boggy nasal turbinates (allergic rhinitis). Oropharynx clear. No lip or tongue swelling.
physicalExam.reasoning: Allergic rhinitis findings. No current angioedema.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing. Good air movement.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal lungs - no asthma, which is reassuring for anaphylaxis prognosis.

physicalExam.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal cardiac exam.

labs.name: Skin Prick Test - Peanut
labs.result: Peanut: 12mm wheal (positive, >3mm = positive). Histamine positive control: 5mm. Saline negative control: 0mm.
labs.reasoning: Positive skin test (12mm) combined with convincing history = high probability of true peanut allergy. Large wheal size correlates with higher likelihood of clinical reactivity.

labs.name: Peanut-specific IgE
labs.result: Peanut sIgE: 45.2 kU/L (high positive). Reference: >15 kU/L associated with >95% likelihood of clinical reaction.
labs.reasoning: High peanut-specific IgE (>15 kU/L) has >95% positive predictive value for clinical allergy. Confirms high likelihood of true allergy.

labs.name: Ara h 2 Component Testing
labs.result: Ara h 2 sIgE: 28.5 kU/L (elevated). Ara h 8: <0.1 kU/L (negative).
labs.reasoning: Ara h 2 is peanut storage protein - positive indicates true peanut allergy. Ara h 8 is PR-10 (cross-reactive with birch) - negative makes oral allergy syndrome unlikely. This is TRUE peanut allergy.

labs.name: Tree Nut Skin Testing Panel
labs.result: Cashew: negative (2mm). Almond: negative (2mm). Walnut: 4mm (borderline). Pecan: 4mm (borderline). Pistachio: negative. Brazil nut: negative. Hazelnut: negative.
labs.reasoning: Cashew and almond negative - consistent with clinical tolerance. Walnut/pecan borderline - consider specific IgE or oral challenge. Most tree nuts negative.

labs.name: Tree Nut Specific IgE (for borderline skin tests)
labs.result: Walnut sIgE: 1.2 kU/L (low positive). Pecan sIgE: 0.8 kU/L (low positive).
labs.reasoning: Low positive IgE to walnut/pecan - lower risk but not zero. Will need oral challenge or continued avoidance with shared decision making.

procedures.name: Oral Food Challenge to Peanut
procedures.result: NOT INDICATED: Clear clinical history + high peanut sIgE + positive Ara h 2 = diagnosis confirmed without OFC. Challenge would pose unnecessary risk.
procedures.reasoning: OFC not needed when history is clear and testing shows high probability of true allergy.

procedures.name: Consider Oral Food Challenge - Cashew
procedures.result: Offered but parents declined - comfortable with cashew given negative testing and clinical tolerance history. Will continue cashew at home.
procedures.reasoning: OFC would definitively confirm tolerance but negative skin test + clinical tolerance is adequate for most families.

treatments.name: Epinephrine Auto-injector Prescription
treatments.result: Prescribed EpiPen Jr (0.15mg) x2. Demonstrated proper use with trainer. Provided written anaphylaxis action plan.
treatments.reasoning: ALL patients with IgE-mediated food allergy need epinephrine auto-injectors. Two devices always carried.

treatments.name: Anaphylaxis Emergency Action Plan
treatments.result: Written action plan provided with signs of mild vs severe reaction, when to give epinephrine, and instruction to always call 911 after epinephrine.
treatments.reasoning: Written plan ensures proper emergency response by family, school, caregivers.

treatments.name: Peanut Avoidance Counseling
treatments.result: Extensive education on: reading labels (may contain warnings), cross-contact in restaurants, high-risk foods, precautionary labeling. Provided written materials.
treatments.reasoning: Avoidance education is cornerstone of management - most reactions are accidental exposures.

treatments.name: Tree Nut Guidance
treatments.result: Based on testing: OK to continue eating cashews, almonds, pistachios. Avoid walnuts and pecans pending shared decision making (low positive IgE). Peanut is a legume, not tree nut.
treatments.reasoning: Individualized tree nut guidance based on testing - not automatic avoidance of all tree nuts.

treatments.name: Oral Immunotherapy (OIT) Discussion
treatments.result: Discussed peanut OIT: Available options include Palforzia (FDA-approved) and clinic-based OIT. Benefits: reduces severity of accidental reactions, may allow tolerance of small amounts. Risks: allergic reactions during treatment (common), daily dosing required, does not cure allergy. Family interested - will schedule dedicated OIT consultation.
treatments.reasoning: OIT is an option for food allergy treatment - families should be informed and given choice.

disposition.name: School Forms and 504 Plan
disposition.result: Completed Food Allergy Action Plan for school. Discussed 504 plan for accommodations (peanut-free table, staff training, field trip protocols). Provided documentation for school nurse.
disposition.reasoning: School safety planning is critical - most accidental exposures occur outside home.

disposition.name: Medical Alert Recommendation
disposition.result: Recommended medical alert bracelet indicating peanut allergy.
disposition.reasoning: Medical ID alerts first responders in emergency.

disposition.name: Follow-up Plan
disposition.result: Return in 1 year to recheck peanut IgE (may trend over time - some decrease suggests possible future tolerance). Sooner if interested in OIT. Annual follow-up for ongoing management.
disposition.reasoning: Annual monitoring - some children outgrow peanut allergy (about 20%), trending IgE helps predict.

actualDiagnosis.name: IgE-Mediated Peanut Allergy
keyFindings: Immediate reaction (hives, vomiting, angioedema) after peanut ingestion
teachingPoints: Diagnosis of IgE-mediated food allergy = convincing history + positive testing
Skin test and specific IgE show SENSITIZATION, not necessarily clinical allergy
Ara h 2 is best component for predicting true peanut allergy (storage protein)
Ara h 8 positive alone suggests oral allergy syndrome (birch-peanut cross-reactivity)
Peanut is a LEGUME, not a tree nut - don't automatically avoid tree nuts
Test tree nuts individually - ~30% co-allergy but not universal
ALL food allergic patients need epinephrine auto-injectors
Oral immunotherapy (OIT) is an option - discuss with interested families
About 20% of peanut allergic children will outgrow - can track with IgE trends

child-8-peanut.teachingPoint: The combination of convincing clinical history (immediate onset multi-system reaction) plus positive testing (skin test + specific IgE + Ara h 2 component) confirms peanut allergy without need for oral food challenge. Always test tree nuts individually - peanut is a legume and automatic tree nut avoidance is not necessary.
child-8-peanut.clinicalPearl: Ara h 2 is the 'gold standard' peanut component for predicting true clinical allergy. Patients positive to Ara h 2 have >95% probability of clinical reactivity. Conversely, patients positive ONLY to Ara h 8 (a PR-10 protein that cross-reacts with birch pollen) often have oral allergy syndrome and can frequently tolerate heated/processed peanut.

historyQuestions.reasoning: KEY: Angioedema WITHOUT urticaria suggests bradykinin-mediated (HAE or ACEi).

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the abdominal attacks.
historyQuestions.answer: Severe cramping, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Sometimes I go to the ER thinking it's appendicitis. They do CT scans showing bowel wall edema but no surgery needed.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Abdominal attacks from bowel wall edema - often misdiagnosed as surgical abdomen.

historyQuestions.text: What helps during attacks?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing. Benadryl, steroids, epinephrine - I've tried everything. It just has to run its course.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No response to antihistamines/steroids = NOT histamine-mediated.

historyQuestions.text: Family history of swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: My father has the same thing. He was hospitalized once when his throat swelled. My grandmother died from throat swelling.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Autosomal dominant inheritance. Family history of laryngeal edema death is tragic but common in undiagnosed HAE.

historyQuestions.text: Any triggers?
historyQuestions.answer: Stress, dental work, my menstrual period, sometimes trauma like bumping my hand.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic triggers: stress, trauma, dental procedures, estrogen fluctuations.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing regularly. No blood pressure medicines.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to rule out ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema.

physicalExam.finding: Normal vitals. Not currently having an attack.
physicalExam.reasoning: Stable between attacks.

physicalExam.finding: No urticaria. No current angioedema. Skin clear.
physicalExam.reasoning: No hives - supports bradykinin-mediated angioedema.

labs.name: C4 Level
labs.result: C4: 4 mg/dL (LOW, normal 16-48). Low even between attacks.
labs.reasoning: C4 is consumed during complement activation. Low C4 between attacks is screening test for HAE.

labs.name: C1-Inhibitor Level
labs.result: C1-INH: 8 mg/dL (LOW, normal 21-39).
labs.reasoning: Low C1-INH level = Type 1 HAE (85% of cases).

labs.name: C1-Inhibitor Function
labs.result: C1-INH function: 25% (LOW, normal >67%).
labs.reasoning: Confirms dysfunctional C1-INH. In Type 2 HAE, level is normal but function is low.

treatments.name: On-Demand Treatment (Icatibant)
treatments.result: Prescribed icatibant (Firazyr) for acute attacks. Self-injectable bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist. Use at first sign of attack.
treatments.reasoning: All HAE patients need on-demand treatment available.

treatments.name: Alternative: C1-INH Concentrate
treatments.result: C1-INH concentrate (Berinert, Cinryze) also available for acute treatment.
treatments.reasoning: Replaces deficient protein. Alternative to icatibant.

treatments.name: Prophylaxis Discussion (Lanadelumab)
treatments.result: Discussed lanadelumab (Takhzyro) for prophylaxis - anti-kallikrein monoclonal antibody, SQ every 2-4 weeks. Reduces attack frequency by 87%.
treatments.reasoning: Prophylaxis indicated for frequent attacks (>1/month) or history of severe attacks.

treatments.name: Pre-Procedure Prophylaxis Protocol
treatments.result: Before dental/surgical procedures: C1-INH concentrate 1-6 hours before. Have on-demand treatment available.
treatments.reasoning: Procedures are major triggers - prophylaxis essential.

treatments.name: Avoid Estrogen-Containing Contraceptives
treatments.result: Counseled to avoid estrogen-containing OCPs (can worsen HAE). Progestin-only or IUD preferred.
treatments.reasoning: Estrogen increases HAE attack frequency.

disposition.name: Family Screening
disposition.result: Recommend C4 and C1-INH testing for father and any siblings/children.
disposition.reasoning: Autosomal dominant - 50% inheritance risk.

disposition.name: Emergency Action Plan
disposition.result: Written plan for laryngeal attacks: use on-demand treatment immediately and go to ER. Laryngeal attacks are life-threatening.
disposition.reasoning: Laryngeal edema can be fatal - needs emergency protocol.

actualDiagnosis.name: Hereditary Angioedema Type 1
teachingPoints: HAE = angioedema WITHOUT urticaria - key distinguishing feature
C4 is low even BETWEEN attacks - best screening test
Type 1 (85%): Low C1-INH level. Type 2 (15%): Normal level, low function
Bradykinin-mediated - antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine DON'T work
All patients need on-demand treatment (icatibant, C1-INH concentrate)
Prophylaxis for frequent/severe attacks (lanadelumab, berotralstat)
Autosomal dominant - screen family members

female-25-hae.teachingPoint: The KEY to diagnosing HAE is recognizing angioedema WITHOUT urticaria that doesn't respond to antihistamines. If you see this pattern plus family history, check C4 immediately.
female-25-hae.clinicalPearl: The 'Rule of No's' for HAE: No urticaria, No response to antihistamines, No response to steroids, No response to epinephrine. When you see these 'No's', think bradykinin-mediated angioedema (HAE or ACE inhibitor).

female-42-contact.oldcartsNarrative: "My hands have been a mess for about 6 months. Red, itchy, cracking, sometimes blisters. It's embarrassing - I'm a hairdresser and clients see my hands all day. It seems worse at work and better when I'm on vacation. I've tried every cream - hydrocortisone, Eucerin, even prescription steroid cream. It helps temporarily but keeps coming back. I wear gloves at work but they make my hands sweat and sometimes seem to make it worse."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the rash.
historyQuestions.answer: Red, dry, cracked skin on my hands, especially between the fingers and on the backs. Sometimes little blisters that ooze. Incredibly itchy - I scratch until it bleeds sometimes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic eczematous dermatitis pattern - vesicles, erythema, scaling, pruritus.

historyQuestions.text: Is it related to work?
historyQuestions.answer: Definitely worse at work. When I took a week vacation, it improved a lot. Within a few days back at work, it flared again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Work-related pattern strongly suggests occupational contact dermatitis.

historyQuestions.text: What do you do as a hairdresser?
historyQuestions.answer: Cutting, coloring, perms, washing hair. I'm exposed to hair dyes, bleach, perm solutions, shampoos, conditioners all day. I wear latex gloves for color work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple potential allergens: PPD in hair dye, thioglycolates in perms, preservatives, fragrances, latex/rubber in gloves.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the gloves you use.
historyQuestions.answer: Latex gloves usually, sometimes the black nitrile ones. They make my hands sweat and sometimes seem to make the rash worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Latex allergy or rubber accelerator allergy possible. Occlusion from gloves worsens irritant dermatitis.

historyQuestions.text: What products do you use at home?
historyQuestions.answer: Regular hand soap, scented lotion, dish soap without gloves. I've tried switching to 'sensitive' products but no improvement.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Sensitive' products often contain fragrance and preservatives - common allergens.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of eczema or allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No eczema as a kid. I have seasonal allergies but no skin problems until this started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No atopic dermatitis history - makes occupational contact dermatitis more likely.

physicalExam.finding: Bilateral hand dermatitis: erythema, scaling, fissures on dorsal hands and web spaces. Some vesicles on fingers. Lichenification from chronic scratching. Worse on dominant hand.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic contact dermatitis distribution - dorsal hands, web spaces, dominant hand worse.

physicalExam.finding: Nails normal. No pitting or oil spots.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal nails argue against psoriasis.

physicalExam.finding: Forearms clear. No dermatitis elsewhere. No involvement of palms (spared).
physicalExam.reasoning: Limited to hands - supports contact etiology rather than systemic dermatitis.

labs.name: Comprehensive Patch Testing (T.R.U.E. Test + Extended Panel)
labs.result: POSITIVE reactions at 48/96 hours: p-Phenylenediamine (PPD) +++, Thiuram mix (rubber) ++, Fragrance mix I ++, Methylisothiazolinone (MI) ++. Negative: Latex, nickel, other standard allergens.
labs.reasoning: Patch testing identifies specific allergens. PPD (hair dye), thiuram (rubber gloves), fragrance, and MI (preservative) are positive - all relevant to occupation.

treatments.name: Allergen Avoidance Counseling
treatments.result: Extensive counseling: Avoid PPD (in permanent hair dyes - use PPD-free alternatives), Avoid rubber gloves with thiuram (switch to vinyl or accelerator-free nitrile), Avoid fragranced products, Avoid products with MI/MCI preservatives.
treatments.reasoning: Avoidance is the only cure. Must identify PPD-free hair dyes, accelerator-free gloves.

treatments.name: Change Glove Type
treatments.result: Switch to vinyl gloves or accelerator-free nitrile (not standard nitrile which contains thiuram). Avoid latex.
treatments.reasoning: Thiuram is rubber accelerator in most latex and nitrile gloves.

treatments.name: Topical Corticosteroids
treatments.result: Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment BID to affected areas for 2-3 weeks for acute flare.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids control inflammation but don't address cause.

treatments.name: Barrier Repair
treatments.result: Fragrance-free, preservative-free emollient (Vanicream, CeraVe) multiple times daily. Avoid hand sanitizers.
treatments.reasoning: Barrier repair is essential for healing and prevention.

disposition.name: Occupational Medicine Referral
disposition.result: Referred to occupational medicine for documentation of work-related dermatitis. May qualify for accommodations or workers' compensation.
disposition.reasoning: Documentation important for workplace accommodations.

disposition.name: Follow-up in 6 Weeks
disposition.result: Return to assess response to allergen avoidance. If not improved, may need extended patch testing panel.
disposition.reasoning: Follow-up ensures successful avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

cases.title: Rash and Fever After New Medication

guidelineReferences.name: RegiSCAR DRESS Diagnostic Criteria
guidelineReferences.citation: Kardaun SH, et al. Br J Dermatol. 2013;169(5):1071-1080
keyPoints: DRESS: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms
Onset 2-8 weeks after starting culprit drug
Features: Fever, rash, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, organ involvement (liver, kidney, lung)
Mortality 10% - mainly from liver failure
Treatment: Stop drug immediately, systemic corticosteroids for severe cases

male-35-dress.name: Marcus Johnson
male-35-dress.chiefComplaint: I have a rash all over my body and high fever. I started a new seizure medication about 3 weeks ago.

missedConsequences.DRESS Syndrome: 10% mortality, mainly hepatic failure. Delayed recognition worsens prognosis.
missedConsequences.SJS/TEN: Even higher mortality if this is SJS/TEN instead of DRESS.
missedConsequences.Sepsis: Must rule out infection but don't delay stopping culprit drug.

commonPitfalls: Assuming it's a simple drug rash and continuing medication
Not recognizing the 2-8 week latency period
Missing internal organ involvement (check LFTs!)
Confusing with viral illness
Not stopping the drug immediately

mcqs.question: Patient started phenytoin 3 weeks ago, now has fever, diffuse rash, facial swelling, and eosinophilia. What is the MOST important immediate action?

options.explanation: WRONG - must stop the culprit drug immediately.
Correct! Stopping the culprit drug is the most critical intervention. DRESS has 10% mortality, mainly from liver failure.
Carbamazepine has high cross-reactivity with phenytoin - contraindicated.
Delay is dangerous - stop drug immediately.

allDiagnoses.name: DRESS Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Aromatic anticonvulsant, 3-week latency, fever, rash, eosinophilia, facial edema.

allDiagnoses.name: SJS/TEN
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must differentiate - look for mucosal involvement, skin detachment.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Exanthem
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can look similar but eosinophilia and drug timing favor DRESS.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Fever and rash - must rule out infection.

male-35-dress.oldcartsNarrative: "I was diagnosed with epilepsy about a month ago after a seizure. They started me on phenytoin 3 weeks ago. About 3-4 days ago I started feeling awful - fever, fatigue, body aches. I thought I had the flu. Then yesterday this rash started on my trunk and spread everywhere. My face feels swollen, especially around my eyes. The rash is red and itchy. My fever hit 103 last night. I feel terrible - the worst I've ever felt."

historyQuestions.text: When exactly did you start phenytoin?
historyQuestions.answer: Exactly 3 weeks ago. I remember because it was right after my neurology appointment.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 2-8 week latency is characteristic of DRESS. 3 weeks is classic.

historyQuestions.text: When did symptoms start?
historyQuestions.answer: Fever and feeling sick started about 4 days ago. Rash started 2 days ago and spread fast.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prodrome of fever/malaise followed by rash is typical DRESS pattern.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the rash.
historyQuestions.answer: Started on my chest and back, then spread to arms, legs, face. Red, bumpy, itchy. Some areas look like they're running together.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Morbilliform rash progressing to confluence is typical.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the facial swelling.
historyQuestions.answer: My eyelids are puffy, my cheeks feel swollen. I look like I've been in a fight.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Facial edema (especially periorbital) is characteristic of DRESS.

historyQuestions.text: Any mouth sores, eye problems, or genital sores?
historyQuestions.answer: My mouth is a little sore but no real ulcers. Eyes are red and watery. No genital sores.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Mucosal involvement (especially erosions) suggests SJS/TEN - more severe.

historyQuestions.text: Any blistering or skin peeling?
historyQuestions.answer: No blisters. The skin isn't peeling off or anything like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absent blistering/detachment argues against SJS/TEN.

historyQuestions.text: Any swollen glands?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, under my jaw and in my neck. They're tender.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Lymphadenopathy is part of DRESS criteria.

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/68, HR 112, RR 18, Temp 103.2°F, SpO2 96%. Febrile, tachycardic.
physicalExam.reasoning: High fever characteristic of DRESS.

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse morbilliform eruption covering >50% BSA. Confluent on trunk. Facial edema prominent, especially periorbital. No bullae, vesicles, or skin detachment. Nikolsky sign NEGATIVE.
physicalExam.reasoning: Morbilliform rash with facial edema. Negative Nikolsky and no detachment argue against SJS/TEN.

physicalExam.finding: Mild lip edema. Mild conjunctival injection. Oral mucosa slightly erythematous but NO erosions or ulcerations. Genitalia not involved.
physicalExam.reasoning: Minimal mucosal involvement - favors DRESS over SJS/TEN.

physicalExam.finding: Cervical, submandibular, and axillary lymphadenopathy. Tender, 1-2 cm nodes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lymphadenopathy is DRESS criterion.

physicalExam.finding: Mild RUQ tenderness. Liver edge palpable 2cm below costal margin.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatomegaly concerning for liver involvement - most dangerous DRESS complication.

labs.name: CBC with Differential
labs.result: WBC 15.2 with 22% eosinophils (AEC 3,300). Atypical lymphocytes present. Hgb 12.8, Plt 142.
labs.reasoning: Eosinophilia (often >1500) and atypical lymphocytes are DRESS hallmarks.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 285, ALT 342, Alk Phos 180, T.Bili 2.1. Hepatocellular pattern.
labs.reasoning: Liver involvement in 80% of DRESS. This is the main cause of mortality.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Cr 1.4 (baseline 0.9). BUN 28. Other electrolytes normal.
labs.reasoning: Mild renal involvement. Can progress - monitor closely.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.result: 0.3 ng/mL (low - argues against bacterial infection).
labs.reasoning: Helps distinguish DRESS from sepsis - both can cause fever and elevated WBC.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.reasoning: Pulmonary involvement can occur in DRESS.

treatments.name: STOP Phenytoin Immediately
treatments.result: Phenytoin discontinued immediately. This is the most critical intervention.
treatments.reasoning: Stopping the culprit drug is #1 priority. Delays worsen prognosis.

treatments.name: Alternative Seizure Medication
treatments.result: Switched to levetiracetam (Keppra) - low cross-reactivity with aromatic anticonvulsants.
treatments.reasoning: Avoid other aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenobarbital) - high cross-reactivity.

treatments.name: Systemic Corticosteroids
treatments.result: Started prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60mg) given liver involvement and severity.
treatments.reasoning: Steroids indicated for severe DRESS (significant organ involvement). Slow taper over weeks.

treatments.name: Supportive Care
treatments.result: IV fluids, monitoring in step-down unit, daily labs.
treatments.reasoning: Close monitoring essential - can progress rapidly.

disposition.name: Admit to Hospital
disposition.result: Admitted to medicine service for monitoring. Consider dermatology and hepatology consults.
disposition.reasoning: DRESS requires inpatient monitoring - 10% mortality.

disposition.name: Document Allergy
disposition.result: Documented phenytoin allergy as 'DRESS Syndrome.' Also document to avoid other aromatic anticonvulsants due to cross-reactivity.
disposition.reasoning: Lifelong avoidance of phenytoin and cross-reactive drugs.

disposition.name: Long-term Follow-up
disposition.result: DRESS can have prolonged course and late autoimmune sequelae (thyroiditis, diabetes). Follow thyroid function for 1-2 years.
disposition.reasoning: Autoimmune complications can develop months after DRESS resolves.

actualDiagnosis.name: DRESS Syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms)
teachingPoints: DRESS = Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms
Classic latency: 2-8 weeks after starting drug
Key features: Fever, rash >50% BSA, facial edema, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, organ involvement
Liver is most commonly affected organ - cause of most deaths
10% mortality - this is a serious condition
Common culprits: aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lamotrigine), allopurinol, sulfonamides
Treatment: STOP DRUG, systemic steroids for severe cases
Long-term: autoimmune sequelae can occur (thyroiditis, diabetes)

male-35-dress.teachingPoint: DRESS should be suspected when a patient develops fever, rash, and eosinophilia 2-8 weeks after starting a high-risk medication. The facial edema is a helpful distinguishing feature. Always check LFTs - liver involvement is the main cause of death.
male-35-dress.clinicalPearl: The 'DRESS triad' to remember: Drug started 2-8 weeks ago + Diffuse Rash with facial Edema + Systemic involvement (fever, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, organ damage). When you see facial edema with a drug rash, think DRESS.

mcqs.question: A 34-year-old woman with asthma has blood eosinophils of 450 cells/μL, FeNO of 45 ppb, and total IgE of 180 IU/mL. She has chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and aspirin sensitivity. Which biologic would be MOST appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Dupilumab (anti-IL4Rα) is ideal for this patient. The triad of asthma, nasal polyps, and aspirin sensitivity suggests AERD, which is driven by type 2 inflammation involving both IL-4 and IL-13 pathways. Dupilumab is FDA-approved for both severe asthma and CRSwNP, making it a dual-purpose choice. While mepolizumab or benralizumab could address eosinophilic asthma, they don't have the same efficacy for nasal polyps. The IgE of 180 is within range for omalizumab, but it's less effective for the polyp component.

mcqs.question: Which biomarker cutoff indicates T2-high asthma and potential biologic eligibility?
mcqs.explanation: Blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL is the threshold indicating T2-high asthma per GINA guidelines. Higher thresholds (≥300-500) predict better response to anti-IL5 biologics. FeNO ≥20 ppb (not 10) suggests T2 inflammation. IgE levels don't define T2 status but are relevant for omalizumab dosing (30-1500 IU/mL).

allDiagnoses.name: Severe Eosinophilic Asthma (T2-High)
allDiagnoses.rationale: High eosinophils (450), elevated FeNO (45), uncontrolled despite high-dose ICS/LABA, exacerbations requiring oral steroids. Classic T2-high phenotype.

allDiagnoses.name: Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease (AERD)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Triad of asthma + nasal polyps + aspirin sensitivity. Would benefit from aspirin desensitization and dupilumab.

allDiagnoses.name: Allergic Asthma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Elevated IgE, positive allergen testing, symptoms worse with allergen exposure. Overlaps with eosinophilic phenotype.

allDiagnoses.name: Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Should be considered with very high IgE (>1000) and eosinophilia. Check Aspergillus-specific IgE and IgG.

allDiagnoses.name: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Severe asthma + very high eosinophils should prompt consideration. Look for neuropathy, skin findings, cardiac involvement.

allDiagnoses.name: Vocal Cord Dysfunction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common comorbidity/mimic. Inspiratory stridor, throat tightness, doesn't respond to bronchodilators. Diagnose with laryngoscopy.

allDiagnoses.name: Non-T2 (Neutrophilic) Asthma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Low eosinophils and FeNO would suggest this. Less responsive to steroids and current biologics except tezepelumab.

oldcartsNarrative.onset: I've had asthma since I was a kid - diagnosed around age 8. It was pretty mild until about 3 years ago when things got a lot worse.
oldcartsNarrative.location: It's my chest - tightness, wheezing. Sometimes I feel it more in my throat too, especially with strong smells.
oldcartsNarrative.duration: The bad spell started about 3 years ago. Before that I just used my rescue inhaler once in a while. Now I'm using it daily, sometimes multiple times.
oldcartsNarrative.character: Chest tightness is the main thing. Then wheezing - I can hear myself breathe at night. Coughing fits too, especially at night and early morning.
oldcartsNarrative.aggravating: Perfumes and cleaning products are terrible. Cold air. When I get a cold, it always goes to my chest. Exercise can trigger it. Oh, and when I took ibuprofen last year for a headache, I had a really bad reaction.
oldcartsNarrative.relieving: My rescue inhaler helps for a bit. Prednisone when I have to take it. Staying in air conditioning in summer.
oldcartsNarrative.timing: Worse at night and early morning. Seasonal too - spring and fall are harder. But honestly it's never fully controlled anymore.
oldcartsNarrative.severity: I'd say 7 out of 10 right now. I've had to go to the ER twice this year. I missed 2 weeks of work. I can't exercise anymore. I'm scared of traveling.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking for your asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: I take Advair 500/50 twice daily - I never miss it. I also have albuterol which I use... probably 4-5 times a day now. My doctor added Singulair about 6 months ago. And I've had to take prednisone bursts 4 times this year - the last one was just 3 weeks ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High-dose ICS/LABA with frequent rescue use and OCS bursts defines severe uncontrolled asthma. ≥2 exacerbations/year = severe asthma per GINA.

historyQuestions.text: Can you show me how you use your inhalers?
historyQuestions.answer: (Demonstrates good technique with Advair Diskus) I've been using these for years - the respiratory therapist at the hospital taught me. I always rinse my mouth after. For my albuterol HFA, I use it with a spacer like they showed me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirming proper technique rules out 'difficult-to-treat' asthma from poor technique. Many patients have suboptimal technique despite instruction.

historyQuestions.text: How consistently do you take your controller medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Every single day, I promise. Morning and night. I have alarms on my phone. I even bring it when I travel. I know some people don't take it regularly, but I've learned the hard way - when I missed doses years ago, I ended up hospitalized.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Good adherence confirms this is truly severe refractory asthma, not 'difficult' asthma from non-adherence. Up to 50% of uncontrolled asthma is from poor adherence.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any nasal symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Oh yes, my nose is always stuffed up. I can't smell anything anymore - lost my sense of smell maybe 5 years ago. I've had two sinus surgeries for polyps but they keep coming back. The ENT said I have chronic sinusitis with nasal polyps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nasal polyps + asthma suggests unified airway disease and T2 inflammation. Anosmia indicates severe CRSwNP. AERD likely given the triad.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about that reaction to ibuprofen.
historyQuestions.answer: It was scary. About an hour after taking it, my nose completely blocked up, I started wheezing badly, and my face got flushed. I had to use my nebulizer at home and almost went to the ER. My allergist told me I probably have 'aspirin-sensitive asthma' and should avoid all NSAIDs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic NSAID-induced respiratory reaction confirms AERD. The triad of asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID sensitivity. Important for treatment selection and counseling.

historyQuestions.text: What are you allergic to?
historyQuestions.answer: Dust mites - that's the big one. Also cats, tree pollen in spring, ragweed in fall. I had allergy testing years ago. I tried allergy shots for 2 years but had to stop because I kept having reactions, and one time I had a pretty bad one and needed epinephrine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple aeroallergen sensitivities confirms allergic component. Failed immunotherapy due to reactions is common in severe asthma. Elevated IgE expected.

historyQuestions.text: What triggers your asthma symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Strong perfumes are terrible - I have to leave if someone sprays something. Cleaning products with bleach. Cold air in winter. When I get any kind of respiratory infection. Exercise used to trigger it but now I've given up exercising. Wine sometimes makes it worse too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Multiple triggers typical of severe asthma. Wine/alcohol exacerbation suggests possible sulfite sensitivity or cross-reactivity in AERD.

historyQuestions.text: How often have you needed prednisone in the past year?
historyQuestions.answer: Four times this year. Each time for about a week, sometimes 10 days. January when I had a cold, March during pollen season, August when I visited my sister who has cats, and October when I got another cold. My doctor is worried about all the steroids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ≥2 OCS bursts/year defines severe asthma. 4 courses indicates very poor control. Cumulative steroid exposure causes bone loss, diabetes, adrenal suppression.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been hospitalized or intubated for asthma?
historyQuestions.answer: Hospitalized twice - once when I was 12, and once 3 years ago when this all got worse. That time I was in the ICU for 2 days. Never intubated, thank God, but that last time was close. The ER doctor said my oxygen was really low.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ICU admission = near-fatal asthma, a risk factor for asthma death. Requires aggressive management with biologics.

historyQuestions.text: How is asthma affecting your daily life?
historyQuestions.answer: It's taken over my life. I can't exercise - I used to run 5Ks. I'm afraid to travel. I've missed work. My husband and I want to have a baby but I'm scared about being pregnant with asthma this bad. I don't even like going out because of perfume and smoke. It's depressing honestly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant quality of life impact justifies biologic therapy. Pregnancy concerns are valid and need addressing - most biologics have safety data.

historyQuestions.text: Does anyone in your family have asthma or allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom has asthma too - she's on a biologic now and doing great. My brother has bad allergies and eczema. My grandmother had asthma and died from it actually, back in the 70s before they had good treatments.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Strong family history of atopy supports T2-high phenotype. Family death from asthma underscores severity. Mother's response to biologics is encouraging.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had eczema or skin problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I had eczema as a baby and toddler - my mom says I was covered in it. It mostly went away but I still get dry patches on my hands sometimes, especially in winter.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of atopic dermatitis completes the atopic triad (eczema, allergies, asthma). Strong indicator of T2-high disease.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any heartburn or reflux?
historyQuestions.answer: Some, yes. I get heartburn maybe a few times a week, worse when I eat late. I take Tums sometimes. My doctor mentioned GERD might be making my asthma worse but I haven't really treated it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GERD is a common asthma comorbidity and can trigger symptoms. Should be treated, though may not dramatically improve asthma control.

historyQuestions.text: How is your sleep?
historyQuestions.answer: Not great. I wake up coughing at least a few times a week. My husband says I snore now too, which I never used to. I'm always tired.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nocturnal symptoms indicate poor control. New snoring should prompt OSA screening - common comorbidity that worsens asthma.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your work environment.
historyQuestions.answer: I'm an elementary school teacher. The classroom is okay, but the art supplies bother me sometimes. Cold and flu season is hard with all the sick kids. The school has old carpet that I think has dust mites. They use strong cleaners too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: School environment has multiple triggers. Not true occupational asthma but environmental management important. May need accommodations.

historyQuestions.text: What's your home environment like?
historyQuestions.answer: We have a 3-bedroom house. No pets - I wanted a cat but can't. We got rid of carpets and put in hardwood floors. I have allergen covers on the mattress and pillows. We have a HEPA air purifier in the bedroom. I've really tried to make it asthma-friendly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient has already implemented environmental controls - rules out 'difficult asthma' from unaddressed environmental triggers.

available.finding: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Speaking in full sentences. Mild cushingoid features noted (moon facies, slight facial plethora) - likely from recurrent oral steroid use.
available.reasoning: Cushingoid features from OCS exposure highlight need for steroid-sparing biologic therapy.

available.finding: BP 118/72, HR 78, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% on room air. Peak flow 280 L/min (65% of predicted 430 L/min for her height).
available.reasoning: Reduced peak flow at baseline indicates airflow limitation even when 'stable'. SpO2 normal at rest but may drop with exertion.

available.finding: Nasal mucosa pale and boggy bilaterally. Bilateral nasal polyps visible in middle meatus. Unable to appreciate odors (confirmed anosmia). Septal deviation to the left, post-surgical changes noted.
available.reasoning: Visible polyps and anosmia indicate severe CRSwNP. Key finding supporting unified airway T2 disease.

available.finding: Bilateral expiratory wheezes, more prominent in lower lung fields. Prolonged expiratory phase. No crackles. Good air movement despite wheezes.
available.reasoning: Active wheezing at baseline indicates persistent inflammation/obstruction despite controller therapy.

available.finding: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
available.reasoning: Rules out cardiac contribution to dyspnea. Important baseline before starting biologics.

available.finding: Mild xerosis on hands. No active dermatitis. Easy bruising noted on forearms. No purpura or vasculitic lesions.
available.reasoning: Bruising from steroid use. No vasculitic findings to suggest EGPA. Xerosis consistent with atopic tendency.

available.finding: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No joint swelling. Sensation intact.
available.reasoning: Absence of neuropathy, purpura, or other EGPA features is reassuring.

laboratory.result: WBC 8.2, Hgb 13.8, Plt 245. Differential: Eosinophils 450 cells/μL (5.5%), otherwise normal.
laboratory.reasoning: Blood eosinophils ≥150 indicates T2-high asthma. 450 is well above threshold and predicts good response to anti-IL5 or anti-IL4R biologics.
Elevated IgE supports allergic component. Level 30-1500 IU/mL with positive allergen test = omalizumab eligible. 385 is in treatable range.
FeNO ≥20 ppb indicates T2/eosinophilic airway inflammation. 45 is significantly elevated, predicting steroid responsiveness and biologic benefit.

laboratory.result: Positive: Dust mite (Class 4), Cat (Class 3), Tree mix (Class 2), Ragweed (Class 3), Grass (Class 1). Negative: Mold, cockroach.
laboratory.reasoning: Confirms perennial (dust mite) and seasonal allergies. Required for omalizumab eligibility. Guides environmental control.

laboratory.result: Aspergillus-specific IgE: Negative. Aspergillus IgG: Negative.
laboratory.reasoning: Rules out ABPA, which should be considered in severe asthma with high eosinophils and elevated IgE. Negative is reassuring.
Normal tryptase rules out systemic mastocytosis, which can present with flushing and anaphylaxis-like symptoms.
Negative ANCA doesn't rule out EGPA (only 40% are ANCA-positive) but reduces probability. Consider if eosinophils very high or systemic symptoms.

laboratory.result: Na 141, K 4.2, Cl 102, CO2 26, BUN 14, Cr 0.8, Glucose 98. All normal.
laboratory.reasoning: Normal baseline. Important pre-biologic baseline. Glucose normal despite steroid use (monitor for steroid-induced diabetes).

imaging.result: Hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms. No infiltrates, masses, or pleural effusion. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Hyperinflation consistent with obstructive disease. Rules out other causes of dyspnea (CHF, mass, infection).

imaging.result: Pan-sinus opacification with bilateral nasal polyposis. Post-surgical changes from prior polypectomies. Lund-Mackay score 22/24.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms severe CRSwNP supporting unified airway T2 disease. Dupilumab would address both asthma and polyps.

procedures.result: Pre-BD: FEV1 2.1L (68% predicted), FVC 3.2L (85%), FEV1/FVC 0.66. Post-BD: FEV1 2.5L (81% predicted) - 19% improvement. Consistent with moderate obstruction with significant bronchodilator response.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms asthma diagnosis with reversible obstruction. Baseline FEV1 <80% indicates persistent airflow limitation. Important for monitoring biologic response.

procedures.result: Not performed - positive bronchodilator response already confirms asthma diagnosis.
procedures.reasoning: Unnecessary when spirometry already shows reversible obstruction. Would be indicated if spirometry normal and diagnosis uncertain.

consultations.result: Followed by pulmonology x 3 years. Multiple medication escalations. Tried tiotropium (minimal benefit). Completed bronchial thermoplasty evaluation - not a candidate due to eosinophilic phenotype.
consultations.reasoning: Confirms extensive prior treatment attempts. Not a thermoplasty candidate supports biologic approach.

treatments.result: APPROPRIATE CHOICE: Given elevated eosinophils, elevated FeNO, and comorbid CRSwNP with AERD, dupilumab is first-line. Blocks IL-4/IL-13, effective for both asthma and nasal polyps. Dosing: 200mg or 300mg SC every 2 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Dupilumab ideal for this patient: addresses asthma, nasal polyps, and potentially atopic symptoms. IL-4Rα blockade covers multiple T2 cytokines.

treatments.result: REASONABLE ALTERNATIVE: Anti-IL5 effective for eosinophilic asthma. Blood eos 450 predicts good response. However, less effective for nasal polyps than dupilumab. 100mg SC every 4 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Mepolizumab would be appropriate for asthma component but dupilumab preferred given CRSwNP comorbidity.

treatments.result: OPTION: Anti-IgE approved for allergic asthma with IgE 30-1500 and positive allergen test (patient qualifies at IgE 385). Dosing based on weight and IgE. However, eosinophilic/polyp phenotype suggests anti-IL4R or anti-IL5 more targeted.
treatments.reasoning: Patient eligible for omalizumab but dupilumab or mepolizumab likely more effective given high eosinophils and polyps.

treatments.result: OPTION: Anti-IL5Rα with direct eosinophil depletion. May have faster onset than mepolizumab. Q4 weeks x3, then Q8 weeks. Same limitation re: polyps.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative anti-eosinophil biologic. Near-complete eosinophil depletion. Some patients prefer less frequent dosing.

treatments.result: OPTION: Anti-TSLP works upstream, effective across phenotypes including T2-low. May be considered if poor response to other biologics. 210mg SC every 4 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: Tezepelumab has broadest efficacy but typically reserved for failures of more targeted biologics or T2-low phenotype.

treatments.result: RECOMMENDED: Given AERD diagnosis, aspirin desensitization followed by daily aspirin maintenance can improve both sinonasal and asthma symptoms. Do after starting biologic when more stable.
treatments.reasoning: Aspirin desensitization is disease-modifying for AERD. Typically done in specialized center. Complements biologic therapy.

treatments.result: Start PPI therapy (omeprazole 20mg daily) and lifestyle modifications. May modestly improve asthma symptoms though effect often limited.
treatments.reasoning: GERD treatment is low-risk intervention. May help, unlikely to transform asthma control alone.

actualDiagnosis.name: Severe Eosinophilic Asthma (T2-High) with AERD and CRSwNP
keyFindings: Uncontrolled despite high-dose ICS/LABA with good adherence/technique
4 OCS courses and 2 ER visits in past year
Total IgE 385 with multiple aeroallergen sensitivities
Nasal polyps with anosmia - severe CRSwNP
NSAID-induced respiratory reaction - confirms AERD triad
teachingPoints: PHENOTYPING IS ESSENTIAL: T2-high (eosinophilic, allergic, or mixed) vs T2-low (neutrophilic, paucigranulocytic) determines biologic selection
T2 BIOMARKERS: Blood eos ≥150, FeNO ≥20, sputum eos ≥2%, elevated IgE with allergen sensitivity
UNIFIED AIRWAY: Asthma + CRSwNP often coexist as T2 inflammatory disease. Dupilumab treats both
AERD TRIAD: Asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID sensitivity. Aspirin desensitization is disease-modifying
CONFIRM BEFORE ESCALATING: 30% of 'difficult' asthma is misdiagnosis, non-adherence, or untreated comorbidities
BIOLOGIC SELECTION: Anti-IL4R (dupilumab) for mixed T2 or polyps; Anti-IL5/5R (mepo/benra) for pure eosinophilic; Anti-IgE (omalizumab) for allergic; Anti-TSLP (tezepelumab) for broad/T2-low

evidenceBasedPearls.source: Holguin F, et al. ERS/ATS Severe Asthma Guidelines. Eur Respir J 2020
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Match biologic mechanism to patient biomarkers: high eos → anti-IL5; high IgE + allergens → anti-IgE; mixed/polyps → anti-IL4R

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Dupilumab for Asthma with Comorbid CRSwNP
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Dupilumab reduces exacerbations by 70% and improves nasal polyp scores
evidenceBasedPearls.source: LIBERTY ASTHMA QUEST trial; SINUS trials
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: When severe asthma coexists with CRSwNP, dupilumab addresses both conditions

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Higher baseline eosinophils predict better response to anti-IL5 biologics
evidenceBasedPearls.source: GINA 2024; Castro M, et al. Lancet Respir Med 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: ≥150 = T2-high eligible for biologics; ≥300 = greater predicted response; ≥500 = excellent predicted response

revisedDifferential.name: Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease
keyFeatures: Classic triad: asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID reaction

revisedDifferential.name: ABPA
keyFeatures: IgE elevated but not extreme (385, not >1000)

revisedDifferential.name: EGPA
keyFeatures: Eosinophils elevated but not extreme (450, not >1500)
ANCA negative (though EGPA can be ANCA-negative)

female-34-uncontrolled.teachingPoint: This case illustrates comprehensive asthma phenotyping. The patient has severe T2-high asthma confirmed by elevated blood eosinophils (450) and FeNO (45), with comorbid CRSwNP and AERD. After confirming diagnosis (spirometry), ruling out difficult-to-treat factors (adherence, technique, comorbidities), and phenotyping with biomarkers, she's an excellent candidate for dupilumab - which uniquely addresses both her asthma and nasal polyps by blocking IL-4/IL-13 signaling. The key lesson: phenotyping drives precision biologic selection.
female-34-uncontrolled.clinicalPearl: BIOLOGIC DECISION FRAMEWORK: (1) Confirm severe asthma diagnosis with spirometry. (2) Rule out 'difficult asthma' - check adherence, technique, comorbidities. (3) Phenotype with blood eosinophils, FeNO, total IgE, specific IgE. (4) T2-high (eos ≥150 OR FeNO ≥20) → biologic eligible. (5) Choose biologic: Pure eosinophilic → mepolizumab/benralizumab; Allergic → omalizumab; Mixed T2 or comorbid CRSwNP → dupilumab; Broad spectrum or T2-low → tezepelumab. (6) Reassess at 4 months - if inadequate response, switch mechanism class.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Has been increasingly frustrated with worsening asthma over the past 3 years despite doing everything right. Recent ER visit was particularly scary and prompted this referral. Husband is supportive but worried. They've postponed having children because of her health.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: Last exacerbation 3 weeks ago triggered by a viral URI. Required 10-day prednisone burst. Still feeling not fully recovered.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Works as a teacher which exposes her to sick children and environmental triggers. Has already modified home extensively. Feels frustrated that her efforts haven't controlled her asthma. Mother's success on biologic gives her hope.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Married to supportive husband. Close relationship with mother who understands the disease. Good relationship with pulmonologist. Has health insurance that covers biologics (confirmed prior authorization pathway).
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Committed to her treatment. Initially hesitant about biologics (needles, cost, 'is it worth it?') but seeing mother's improvement convinced her. Wants to get pregnant soon and worried about asthma during pregnancy.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Two ER visits this year, both for severe exacerbations. One required ICU admission. Has an asthma action plan but exacerbations happen despite following it.
patientBackground.occupation: Elementary school teacher for 10 years. Has missed significant work due to asthma. School is accommodating but she feels guilty about absences.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

Initiating in patients with uncontrolled asthma
Not providing epinephrine for home SLIT patients
Stopping immunotherapy too early (need 3-5 years for disease modification)

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

ivig-scig-primary.name: Patient
ivig-scig-primary.chiefComplaint: I keep getting pneumonia every few months
ivig-scig-primary.variantDescription: A 35-year-old woman has had 4 episodes of bacterial pneumonia and 2 sinus infections in the past year. She was previously healthy. Labs show IgG 180 mg/dL (normal 700-1600), IgA <7 mg/dL, IgM 15 mg/dL. She has been diagnosed with CVID.
keyFindings: Poor vaccine responses (S. pneumoniae titers low post-vaccination)
ivig-scig-primary.correctDx: Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) requiring immunoglobulin replacement
commonPitfalls: Starting IVIG without checking IgA levels first (anaphylaxis risk)
Using sucrose-containing IVIG in patients with renal insufficiency
Not checking vaccine responses before diagnosing CVID
Targeting a specific IgG number rather than clinical response
